questions.prompt: The branch of biology that names, describes, and organizes living things into groups based on shared characteristics.
questions.explanation: Taxonomy is the science of classifying organisms. Carl Linnaeus founded modern taxonomy in 1753 with Species Plantarum, grouping organisms by shared physical features. Today, taxonomists also use DNA and protein sequences as molecular evidence. The Linnaean hierarchy runs Domain > Kingdom > Phylum > Class > Order > Family > Genus > Species. On NY Living Environment Regents Part A, taxonomy questions ask which organisms are most closely related when shown a classification chart or cladogram. Closer placement on a cladogram (or more shared categories) signals a more recent common ancestor.

questions.prompt: The two-word Latin naming system, in which each species gets a Genus name and species name (e.g., Homo sapiens).
questions.explanation: Binomial nomenclature was standardized by Carl Linnaeus in his 1753 work Species Plantarum. Every species gets a two-part Latin name: the Genus (capitalized, italicized) and the species epithet (lowercase, italicized). Humans = Homo sapiens; domestic cat = Felis catus. The system is universal across all languages, which prevents the confusion of common names — for instance, the 'robin' in Europe is a different species (Erithacus rubecula) than the 'robin' in America (Turdus migratorius). The Regents tests this on Part A as a vocabulary item and on Part B in chart-reading questions.

questions.prompt: A step-by-step paired-choice tool a biologist uses in the field to identify an unknown organism.
questions.explanation: 'Dichotomous' means 'cut in two.' At each step the key presents two contrasting choices — for example, 'leaves needle-like' OR 'leaves broad and flat' — and the user follows whichever fits the specimen to the next pair of choices, eventually arriving at a species name. Dichotomous keys appear regularly on NY Living Environment Regents Part D lab questions, especially in the Relationships and Biodiversity lab where students use morphological and molecular evidence to determine which of four plant species is most closely related to a target species.

questions.prompt: A branching diagram showing evolutionary relationships, where shared branch points represent common ancestors.
questions.explanation: A cladogram is built from shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies) — features inherited from the most recent common ancestor of a group. The deeper the shared branch point (node), the more recent the common ancestor; the closer two species sit on a cladogram, the more recently they diverged. Modern cladograms are increasingly built from DNA and protein-sequence data rather than just morphology. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 frequently presents a cladogram and asks 'Which two organisms share the most recent common ancestor?' — the answer is always the pair joined at the highest (most recent) node.

questions.prompt: The shared evolutionary origin revealed when two species have similar DNA sequences, homologous bones, or matching embryonic development.
questions.explanation: Common ancestry is the foundational claim of Darwin's On the Origin of Species (1859): all life on Earth descends from a shared ancestral population. Evidence comes from four classic sources — (1) homologous structures (same bones, different functions: human arm, whale flipper, bat wing); (2) embryological similarity (all vertebrate embryos have pharyngeal pouches and tails); (3) the fossil record; (4) molecular evidence (humans and chimpanzees share ~98.8% of their DNA). NY Living Environment Regents uses common-ancestry questions to connect classification, evolution, and biochemistry — students must explain how DNA-base-sequence comparison supports placing two species on the same branch of a cladogram.

questions.prompt: The life function in which an organism takes in materials it needs to survive — food in animals, raw materials for photosynthesis in plants.
questions.explanation: Nutrition is one of the eight life functions tracked by the NY Living Environment curriculum. Heterotrophs (animals, fungi, most bacteria) get nutrients by consuming other organisms or their products; autotrophs (plants, algae, cyanobacteria) build their own nutrients from CO2 and water using light energy in photosynthesis. The raw materials produced or absorbed during nutrition are used to build cells, store energy, and run all other life functions. Regents Part A often asks students to match a life function to a specific organ system, and Part B asks students to interpret diagrams showing how nutrients move from digestion into the bloodstream.

questions.prompt: The life function that moves materials such as oxygen, nutrients, and wastes around an organism's body.
questions.explanation: Transport in humans is carried out by the circulatory system — the heart, blood vessels, and blood — which moves oxygen from the lungs to every cell, nutrients from the digestive tract to every cell, and CO2 plus wastes back to the lungs and kidneys. In plants, transport occurs through xylem (water and minerals up from roots) and phloem (sugars from leaves to the rest of the plant). Inside individual cells, transport occurs across the cell membrane by diffusion, osmosis, and active transport. On NY Living Environment Regents Part B, transport often appears in homeostasis diagrams that show how feedback between body systems maintains a stable internal environment.

questions.prompt: The life function that releases energy from food molecules, usually by using oxygen, to make ATP.
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration is the chemical process: glucose + oxygen yields carbon dioxide + water + ATP. It happens primarily in the mitochondria of eukaryotic cells. Aerobic respiration (with oxygen) makes ~36 ATP per glucose; anaerobic respiration (without oxygen) only makes about 2 ATP per glucose. NY Living Environment Regents Part A and Part B routinely test the equation, the role of mitochondria, and the contrast with photosynthesis. A common Part B-2 question asks students to read a graph of CO2 release vs. temperature or O2 availability and explain how the data demonstrate respiration.

questions.prompt: The life function that removes metabolic wastes such as carbon dioxide, urea, and excess water from an organism.
questions.explanation: Excretion gets rid of metabolic waste — products generated by cellular reactions. In humans, the lungs excrete CO2; the kidneys excrete nitrogenous waste (urea) and excess water and salts as urine; the skin excretes water, salt, and small amounts of urea through sweat; the liver processes nitrogen from broken-down amino acids into urea. Excretion is distinct from digestion's elimination of undigested food, which is egestion. NY Living Environment Regents commonly asks students to distinguish excretion from egestion and to identify the kidney as the main excretory organ in a diagram of the urinary system.

questions.prompt: The life function — sometimes called coordination — by which an organism keeps its internal environment stable in response to external change.
questions.explanation: Regulation in humans is carried out by the nervous and endocrine systems, working together in negative-feedback loops to maintain homeostasis — for example, keeping body temperature near 37 degrees Celsius, blood glucose near 90 mg/dL, and blood pH near 7.4. When a stimulus pushes the body away from its set point, a receptor detects it, the nervous or endocrine system sends a signal, and an effector (muscle or gland) responds to bring the variable back. NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely tests this in diagrams of blood-sugar regulation by insulin and glucagon, or sweating and shivering in response to temperature change.

questions.prompt: The class of biological molecules that includes sugars and starches — the cell's main quick-energy source.
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are made of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen in a 1:2:1 ratio. Simple sugars (monosaccharides) such as glucose are the cell's preferred fuel; cellular respiration breaks down glucose to release ATP. Two simple sugars bonded together form a disaccharide (sucrose = glucose + fructose); many bonded together form a polysaccharide (starch in plants, glycogen in animals, cellulose in plant cell walls). NY Living Environment Regents Part A tests the names of the building blocks; Part B uses indicator-test data (Benedict's solution for sugars, iodine for starch) to identify carbohydrates.

questions.prompt: The class of nonpolar biological molecules including fats, oils, waxes, and steroids; the main component of cell membranes.
questions.explanation: Lipids include triglycerides (fats and oils used for long-term energy storage), phospholipids (the phospholipid bilayer of every cell membrane), and steroids (cholesterol, the sex hormones estrogen and testosterone). They are nonpolar — they do not dissolve in water — which is why oil and water separate, and which is why the phospholipid bilayer forms automatically when phospholipids meet water. NY Living Environment Regents Part A asks students to identify lipid functions; Part B may show a phospholipid bilayer diagram and ask which substances can pass through it without help (small, nonpolar molecules can diffuse; large or charged molecules require transport proteins).

questions.prompt: The class of biological molecules built from amino acids — they catalyze reactions, transport oxygen, and form most cellular structures.
questions.explanation: Proteins are polymers of amino acids; 20 different amino acids exist, and the specific sequence determines the protein's three-dimensional shape, which in turn determines its function. Examples include enzymes (lactase, amylase), transport proteins (hemoglobin carrying O2), structural proteins (keratin in hair, collagen in connective tissue), and hormones (insulin). The shape of a protein is everything — high temperatures or extreme pH can denature the protein (unfold it) and destroy function. NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly tests this with enzyme-activity graphs showing optimum temperature/pH and the drop in activity when conditions move away from optimum.

questions.prompt: The class of biological molecules that store and transmit genetic information — DNA and RNA.
questions.explanation: Nucleic acids are polymers built of nucleotides; each nucleotide has a phosphate group, a 5-carbon sugar, and a nitrogenous base. In DNA the sugar is deoxyribose and the bases are A, T, G, C (A pairs with T, G pairs with C). In RNA the sugar is ribose and U replaces T. DNA stores the cell's hereditary instructions; messenger RNA carries those instructions to ribosomes, which translate them into proteins. Watson and Crick published the double-helix structure of DNA in 1953, building on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography image (Photo 51). NY Living Environment Regents Part A tests the base-pairing rule and Part B tests transcription/translation diagrams.

questions.prompt: Protein catalysts that speed up biochemical reactions by lowering the activation energy; lactase and amylase are examples.
questions.explanation: Enzymes are proteins whose three-dimensional shape includes a region called the active site that binds a specific substrate. This shape-specific binding gives the lock-and-key model. Each enzyme has an optimum temperature and pH — outside that range the protein denatures, the active site changes shape, and activity drops. Examples: amylase in saliva breaks down starch; lactase breaks down lactose; pepsin in the stomach (optimum pH ~2) breaks down proteins. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses enzyme graphs constantly: students must read the optimum temperature/pH, predict what happens above and below it, and explain the result with a sentence about denaturation and the active site changing shape.

questions.prompt: The light microscope used in every NY Living Environment classroom — has two sets of lenses (ocular and objective) that magnify together.
questions.explanation: A compound microscope uses two lenses in series. Total magnification = ocular magnification (usually 10x) multiplied by the objective magnification (typically 4x, 10x, 40x, or 100x), giving 40x up to 1000x. Compound microscopes are standard equipment in every NY Living Environment lab. They are required on Regents Part D, where students must label parts (stage, objective lenses, coarse and fine focus, diaphragm), calculate total magnification, and interpret what they see in the field of view. They view living and stained cells — Elodea leaf cells, onion epidermis, cheek cells, paramecium, and the diffusion through a membrane lab.

questions.prompt: The microscope-slide preparation in which a specimen is placed in a drop of water and topped with a cover slip — used for viewing live cells like Elodea or paramecium.
questions.explanation: A wet mount slide is prepared by placing the specimen in a small drop of water on a glass slide, then lowering a thin cover slip at an angle to avoid trapping air bubbles. Wet mounts are required to view live organisms (paramecium, daphnia, elodea), to use stains, and to perform the NYSED Diffusion Through a Membrane lab where students stain plant cells. Common Part D test items show a diagram of a wet mount with an air bubble and ask the student to (a) identify the problem and (b) describe how to fix it (re-lower the cover slip at an angle, or tap the cover slip lightly).

questions.prompt: A colored chemical (such as iodine or methylene blue) added to a specimen on a slide to make cell structures easier to see.
questions.explanation: Stains are dyes that bind selectively to cell parts to increase contrast under the microscope. Methylene blue is commonly used on cheek cells to darken the nucleus; iodine stain (Lugol's solution) is used both to make plant cell walls and nuclei visible, and to indicate starch (turns blue-black in the presence of starch). In the NYSED Diffusion Through a Membrane lab, Lugol's outside a model 'cell' (dialysis tubing) diffuses inward, turning a starch solution blue-black, demonstrating movement of small iodine molecules across a selectively permeable membrane while starch stays trapped inside.

questions.prompt: The increase in apparent size of a specimen seen under a microscope, calculated by multiplying the ocular by the objective.
questions.explanation: Total magnification on a compound light microscope = ocular magnification (usually 10x) multiplied by objective magnification (commonly 4x, 10x, 40x, or 100x). A 10x ocular with a 40x objective gives 400x total. Higher magnification narrows the field of view (less area visible) and reduces the depth of field. Light microscopes max out around 1000x because of the wavelength of visible light; electron microscopes reach over 1,000,000x. NY Living Environment Regents Part D regularly asks students to compute total magnification, and to predict what happens to the field of view when they switch from low to high power.

questions.prompt: The circle of light visible through the eyepiece of a microscope — narrower at high power, wider at low power.
questions.explanation: The field of view is the visible circle a student sees through the ocular. It is wider on low-power objectives and narrower on high-power objectives. Students estimate cell size by using a transparent ruler at low power to measure the field's diameter (e.g., 1.5 mm = 1500 micrometers), then counting how many cells fit across the field at the same magnification. If 10 cells fit across a 1500 micrometer field, each cell is ~150 micrometers wide. NY Living Environment Regents Part D test items routinely require students to estimate cell size by this method.

questions.prompt: Body parts in different species that have the same underlying bone structure but may serve different functions — like the human arm, whale flipper, and bat wing.
questions.explanation: Homologous structures share the same internal skeletal arrangement — humerus, radius, ulna, carpals, metacarpals, phalanges — even though they serve different functions (gripping in humans, swimming in whales, flying in bats). Same blueprint, different use, inherited from a common ancestor. Darwin used homologous structures in On the Origin of Species (1859) as some of the earliest evidence for common descent. On NY Living Environment Regents Part B, students compare diagrams of forelimbs across species and must explain that the similar bone structure suggests the species share a common ancestor — Regents-style answer: 'The similar bones indicate the species evolved from a common ancestor.'

questions.prompt: Body parts in different species that look similar and do similar jobs but evolved independently — like the wings of a bird and an insect.
questions.explanation: Analogous structures look alike and do similar jobs but are NOT inherited from a common ancestor; they evolved separately in response to similar environmental pressures (convergent evolution). Classic examples: a bird wing (modified vertebrate forelimb with feathers) vs. an insect wing (an outgrowth of the exoskeleton); a shark's fin vs. a dolphin's fin (one is a fish, the other is a mammal). NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to distinguish homologous from analogous structures — homologous = same ancestor, analogous = same environment. Only homologous structures are evidence of common ancestry.

questions.prompt: The variety of different species in a particular ecosystem, biome, or on Earth as a whole.
questions.explanation: Biodiversity is the variety of life — measured at the level of genetic diversity within a species, species diversity within an ecosystem, and ecosystem diversity across a region. High biodiversity makes ecosystems more resilient to disturbance (disease, climate change) because if one species is wiped out, others can fill its niche. NYSED's required Relationships and Biodiversity lab has students compare four plant species to a valuable medicinal plant using morphology, DNA, and protein evidence to decide which is most closely related — directly testing biodiversity as a Regents Part D topic.

questions.prompt: An inherited trait that helps an organism survive and reproduce in its environment — like the thick fur of an Arctic fox.
questions.explanation: An adaptation is an inherited trait that increases an organism's fitness in its environment — its ability to survive and reproduce. Adaptations arise when natural selection acts on variation in a population over generations. Classic examples: the beak shapes of the Galapagos finches, each suited to a different food source (Darwin's observations on the HMS Beagle 1831-1836); thick fur on Arctic mammals; camouflage coloration in moths and chameleons. Adaptations do NOT occur in individuals during their lifetime — they evolve across generations. NY Living Environment Regents Part B always tests this misconception with questions where individual giraffes do NOT 'stretch their necks longer'; rather, the long-necked variants survive and reproduce more.

questions.prompt: The differences in heritable traits within a population — the raw material on which natural selection acts.
questions.explanation: Variation among individuals in a population comes from sources including mutation, sexual reproduction (independent assortment and crossing over in meiosis), and random fertilization. Without variation, natural selection has nothing to act on and a population cannot evolve. In Darwin's Galapagos finches, beak-size variation existed within each species; drought conditions favored the larger-beaked birds, and within a single generation the average beak size in the population increased. NY Living Environment Regents Part B questions tied to the Beaks of Finches lab use this exact pattern: identify the source of variation, then explain how environmental pressure selects for some variants over others.

questions.prompt: The selectively permeable outer boundary of all cells, made of a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins.
questions.explanation: The cell membrane (plasma membrane) is the boundary that controls what enters and exits a cell. It is built from a phospholipid bilayer studded with transport proteins, receptor proteins, and cholesterol. Small, nonpolar molecules (O2, CO2) diffuse straight through; large, polar, or charged molecules (glucose, ions) require transport proteins. The fluid-mosaic model (Singer and Nicolson 1972) describes the membrane as a fluid sea of lipids with mosaic proteins moving within it. The Regents Part B regularly tests the membrane in diagrams of diffusion, osmosis, active transport, and the NYSED Diffusion Through a Membrane lab using dialysis tubing as a model cell.

questions.prompt: The two-layered arrangement of phospholipid molecules — heads outside, tails inside — that forms the core of every cell membrane.
questions.explanation: Each phospholipid has a polar (hydrophilic, water-loving) head and two nonpolar (hydrophobic, water-fearing) fatty-acid tails. In water, phospholipids spontaneously self-assemble so that the heads face the watery outside and inside of the cell, while the tails point inward toward each other — creating a two-layer sheet (a bilayer). This arrangement makes the membrane a selective barrier: small, nonpolar molecules pass through the lipid core easily; charged or large molecules need transport proteins to cross. NY Living Environment Regents Part A asks for the structural pattern; Part B shows the bilayer in cross-section and asks which substances cross by diffusion.

questions.prompt: The property of the cell membrane that lets it pick and choose which substances pass through.
questions.explanation: Selective permeability (semi-permeability) means the membrane allows some molecules to cross while blocking others. Small uncharged molecules — O2, CO2, urea, water — cross easily by diffusion or facilitated diffusion. Large, polar, or charged molecules — glucose, ions, amino acids — need specific transport proteins. The NYSED Diffusion Through a Membrane lab models this exactly: dialysis tubing acts as a selectively permeable membrane that lets small iodine molecules (Lugol's stain) cross but blocks larger starch molecules. NY Living Environment Regents Part D commonly tests this lab with a question on which substance(s) crossed and how the data prove it.

questions.prompt: The net movement of small molecules from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration — no ATP required.
questions.explanation: Diffusion is passive transport: molecules move from where they are more concentrated to where they are less concentrated, down their concentration gradient, without the cell spending ATP. Oxygen diffuses from alveoli (high O2) into the bloodstream (low O2); CO2 diffuses from cells (high CO2) into the blood. Diffusion stops at dynamic equilibrium, when molecules are equally distributed and net movement is zero (though individual molecules still move). The NYSED Diffusion Through a Membrane lab and Regents Part B diagrams use this concept constantly — students must identify direction of diffusion based on the concentration gradient shown.

questions.prompt: The diffusion of water specifically across a selectively permeable membrane, from where water is more concentrated (low solute) to where water is less concentrated (high solute).
questions.explanation: Osmosis is a special case of diffusion — water moving across a selectively permeable membrane down its concentration gradient. In a hypotonic solution (less solute outside than inside the cell), water flows into the cell; animal cells swell and can burst, while plant cells become turgid. In a hypertonic solution (more solute outside), water flows out; cells shrink (crenate in animals, plasmolyze in plants). In an isotonic solution, water moves in and out equally. NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly tests osmosis with diagrams of red blood cells or Elodea cells in different solutions and asks students to predict the direction of water movement.

questions.prompt: The membrane-bound organelle in every eukaryotic cell that contains the DNA and serves as the control center.
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the largest and most visible organelle in most eukaryotic cells. It is enclosed by a double-membrane (nuclear envelope) studded with nuclear pores that allow mRNA and proteins to move in and out. Inside the nucleus, DNA is organized as chromatin (loose during interphase) or chromosomes (condensed during cell division). The nucleolus, a dark spot inside the nucleus, manufactures ribosomes. NY Living Environment Regents Part A asks for the nucleus's function; Part B and Part D show photographs or diagrams of stained cells (cheek cells stained with methylene blue) where the nucleus appears as a dark central oval and students must identify it.

questions.prompt: The tiny organelle, made of RNA and protein, that builds proteins by linking amino acids in the order specified by messenger RNA.
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the protein-building machines of the cell. They read mRNA three bases at a time (a codon), match each codon to a tRNA carrying the right amino acid, and link the amino acids into a growing polypeptide chain — this is translation. Ribosomes float free in the cytoplasm (making proteins for the cell's own use) or attach to the rough endoplasmic reticulum (making proteins for export, like digestive enzymes or insulin). Both prokaryotes and eukaryotes have ribosomes, which is one piece of evidence that all life shares a common ancestor. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses translation diagrams with ribosomes constantly.

questions.prompt: The 'powerhouse' organelle where most cellular respiration takes place, producing ATP from glucose and oxygen.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria (plural) are double-membrane organelles where most of the cell's ATP is produced by aerobic cellular respiration. The inner membrane is folded into cristae, increasing surface area for the electron transport chain. Mitochondria have their own DNA and ribosomes — Lynn Margulis proposed in 1967 (endosymbiotic theory) that mitochondria descend from a free-living aerobic bacterium that was engulfed by an ancestral eukaryotic cell. Active cells (muscle, liver, neurons) contain hundreds to thousands of mitochondria. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses mitochondria in diagrams of cellular respiration and asks how mitochondrial number relates to a cell's energy needs.

questions.prompt: The green organelle, found only in plant and algal cells, where photosynthesis converts light energy into glucose.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, the green pigment that absorbs light energy. They use that energy plus CO2 and H2O to produce glucose and release O2 — photosynthesis. Like mitochondria, chloroplasts have a double membrane, their own DNA, and their own ribosomes; Lynn Margulis's endosymbiotic theory (1967) holds that chloroplasts evolved from free-living photosynthetic cyanobacteria engulfed by ancient eukaryotes. The inner membranes (thylakoids) stack into grana. Chloroplasts are found in plant cells and algal cells but NOT in animal or fungal cells. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses chloroplast diagrams in photosynthesis questions, often paired with mitochondria in respiration diagrams.

questions.prompt: The large fluid-filled storage organelle that gives plant cells turgor pressure when full of water.
questions.explanation: A vacuole is a membrane-bound sac used for storage (of water, salts, sugars, proteins, wastes). Plant cells have one very large central vacuole that fills most of the cell volume and presses outward against the cell wall, creating turgor pressure that keeps the plant rigid and upright; when a plant wilts, those vacuoles have lost water. Animal cells have many small vacuoles. Paramecium and other freshwater protists have contractile vacuoles that pump out excess water entering the cell by osmosis. NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to identify the central vacuole in plant cell diagrams and to explain how loss of vacuole water causes wilting.

questions.prompt: The three-part scientific principle that (1) all living things are made of cells, (2) cells are the basic unit of life, and (3) all cells come from preexisting cells.
questions.explanation: Cell theory is the unifying foundation of biology. Robert Hooke first observed and named 'cells' in 1665 in slices of cork under his microscope (Micrographia). Anton van Leeuwenhoek observed living single-celled organisms ('animalcules') in the 1670s. In 1839, Matthias Schleiden (plants) and Theodor Schwann (animals) proposed that all living things are made of cells. Rudolf Virchow in 1855 added 'omnis cellula e cellula' (all cells come from cells) — completing the theory. Louis Pasteur later disproved spontaneous generation, confirming Virchow's claim. NY Living Environment Regents Part A and B regularly test the three parts of cell theory and the contributors.

questions.prompt: An organism made of just one cell, such as a paramecium or an Amoeba.
questions.explanation: A unicellular organism is one whose entire body is a single cell, which carries out all eight life functions inside that one cell. Examples include bacteria (prokaryotes), archaea, and many protists — paramecium, amoeba, euglena. Most life on Earth, by number of organisms, is unicellular. Single-celled organisms reproduce by binary fission or other forms of asexual reproduction, completing a generation in minutes or hours. NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly contrasts unicellular and multicellular organisms in life-function diagrams, especially in questions about how a paramecium accomplishes nutrition, excretion, and regulation with no specialized organ systems.

questions.prompt: An organism made of many cells specialized to perform different functions — like a human, an oak tree, or a mushroom.
questions.explanation: A multicellular organism is one whose body has many cells that work together. Multicellularity allowed cell specialization (differentiation): different cells become muscle, nerve, blood, bone, leaf, or root cells, each excellent at one job. Specialized cells form tissues, which form organs, which form organ systems, which form the whole organism. Multicellularity evolved independently several times in Earth's history — in animals, plants, fungi, brown and red algae. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses the levels-of-organization diagram (cell-tissue-organ-system-organism) and asks students to identify the proper sequence.

questions.prompt: The ratio that explains why cells stay small — as a cell gets bigger, its volume increases faster than its surface area.
questions.explanation: A cell exchanges materials (nutrients in, wastes out) across its surface. As cell volume doubles, surface area increases less than that — the surface area to volume ratio (SA:V) drops. If a cell gets too big, the membrane cannot exchange materials fast enough to support the interior, and the cell will die or must divide. This is why cells are mostly microscopic. Cells with high exchange demands often have shape adaptations to keep SA:V high: long thin neurons, microvilli on intestinal cells, root hairs on plant roots, flat thin alveoli in lungs. NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly asks students to predict which of two cells will exchange faster based on SA:V.

questions.prompt: The smallest distance at which a microscope can distinguish two separate points; limits how much detail you can see.
questions.explanation: Resolution is the ability to tell two close points apart. The light microscope is limited by the wavelength of visible light (~400-700 nm) and can resolve down to about 200 nm — enough to see most organelles but not detailed structure within them. Electron microscopes (TEM, SEM) use electron beams with much shorter wavelengths and resolve down to ~0.1 nm, allowing scientists to see individual large molecules, viruses, and the cristae inside mitochondria. NY Living Environment Regents Part D tests this distinction — light microscope shows cells and organelles; electron microscope shows fine structure inside organelles and individual viruses.

questions.prompt: Movement of substances across the cell membrane against the concentration gradient, requiring ATP and a transport protein.
questions.explanation: Active transport moves substances from low to high concentration — uphill — and requires energy (ATP) and a transport protein. The classic example is the sodium-potassium pump in nerve and muscle cells, which pumps 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per ATP. Plant roots use active transport to pull mineral ions (nitrate, potassium) from soil where concentrations are lower than inside the root. Endocytosis and exocytosis (the cell forming a vesicle to engulf or expel material) are also forms of active transport. NY Living Environment Regents Part B distinguishes active transport from diffusion using gradient direction and ATP need.

questions.prompt: Movement of substances across the cell membrane down their concentration gradient, no ATP needed.
questions.explanation: Passive transport moves substances from high concentration to low concentration — downhill — with no ATP required. The three types are: (1) simple diffusion (small nonpolar molecules slip through the lipid bilayer — O2, CO2); (2) facilitated diffusion (larger or polar molecules pass through a channel or carrier protein — glucose, ions); (3) osmosis (water moves across a selectively permeable membrane). NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly asks students to read a diagram of a membrane with arrows and identify whether each movement is passive or active, based on gradient direction and presence/absence of ATP.

questions.prompt: The difference in the amount of a substance between two regions — the driving force for diffusion.
questions.explanation: A concentration gradient exists when a substance is more concentrated in one region than another. Molecules naturally diffuse down a concentration gradient (high to low) until evenly distributed (dynamic equilibrium). The steeper the gradient, the faster the diffusion rate. In the lungs, oxygen is more concentrated in alveoli than in the capillaries, so O2 diffuses into the blood; CO2 is more concentrated in the blood than in alveoli, so CO2 diffuses out. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses concentration-gradient arrows on diagrams of cell membranes, alveoli, and root cells, asking students to predict the direction of net movement.

questions.prompt: The internal pressure plant cells generate when their central vacuole is full of water — what keeps a plant from wilting.
questions.explanation: When a plant cell is in a hypotonic environment (less solute outside than inside), water flows into the central vacuole by osmosis, which presses outward against the rigid cell wall — this is turgor pressure. Turgor pressure keeps non-woody plants upright; when soil is dry and cells lose water, turgor pressure drops and the plant wilts. Place a wilted celery stalk in fresh water and turgor returns. NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly tests this with experimental data showing plant mass change in different sugar/salt solutions and asks students to explain the water movement by osmosis and the resulting turgor change.

questions.prompt: The balanced state of life — molecules constantly entering and leaving, but no net change overall; basis of homeostasis.
questions.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium is a state where forward and reverse processes occur at equal rates, so there is no net change — but molecules are still moving constantly. In diffusion, dynamic equilibrium means molecules are evenly distributed but each individual molecule continues to move randomly. At the organism level, dynamic equilibrium underlies homeostasis: blood glucose, body temperature, and blood pH stay within narrow ranges through constant feedback adjustments. NY Living Environment Regents Part A names this as the goal of homeostasis; Part B uses it in graphs where a variable wobbles slightly around a set point.

questions.prompt: An unspecialized cell that can divide and develop into many different cell types — a major focus of medical biotechnology.
questions.explanation: Stem cells are undifferentiated cells with two key properties: they can divide repeatedly (self-renewal), and they can develop into specialized cell types (potency). Embryonic stem cells (pluripotent) can become any cell type in the body. Adult stem cells (multipotent) are more limited — bone marrow stem cells produce all blood-cell types. Stem-cell research aims to replace damaged tissue (heart muscle after attack, nerve cells after spinal injury, pancreas cells in diabetes). NY Living Environment Regents Part A tests the definition; Part B regularly asks students to evaluate biotechnology applications including stem-cell therapies and the ethical and biological tradeoffs.

questions.prompt: The process by which unspecialized cells become specialized to perform a specific role — muscle cell, nerve cell, leaf cell.
questions.explanation: Differentiation is the process in which an unspecialized cell becomes specialized — a stem cell becoming a red blood cell, a neuron, a liver cell. Every body cell contains the same complete genome, but differentiation turns on different sets of genes in different cells, producing different proteins and therefore different cell structure and function. Differentiation is regulated by chemical signals from neighboring cells and the surrounding environment. NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly tests this distinction: 'How can two cells have the same DNA but look and function differently?' — Answer: different genes are expressed (turned on) in each cell type.

questions.prompt: A group of similar specialized cells that work together to perform one function — like muscle tissue or epithelial tissue.
questions.explanation: A tissue is a group of similar cells with the same specialization that work together. Animals have four basic tissue types — epithelial (covers surfaces and lines cavities), connective (blood, bone, fat, cartilage), muscle (skeletal, smooth, cardiac), and nervous (neurons and supporting cells). Plant tissues include dermal (epidermis), vascular (xylem and phloem), and ground tissue. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses the levels-of-organization sequence — cells form tissues, tissues form organs, organs form organ systems, organ systems form the organism — and requires students to place a structure (e.g., 'heart muscle' or 'leaf epidermis') at the right level.

questions.prompt: A structure made of two or more tissue types working together for one function — like the heart, stomach, kidney, or leaf.
questions.explanation: An organ is a body part with a distinct structure that combines multiple tissue types to carry out a specific function. The heart contains cardiac muscle (pumps blood), connective tissue (holds the structure together), epithelial tissue (lines chambers and valves), and nervous tissue (controls rhythm). A leaf contains dermal tissue (waxy cuticle and epidermis), vascular tissue (xylem and phloem), and ground tissue (mesophyll where photosynthesis occurs). NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests the levels of organization with diagrams and asks students to identify an organ as the next step above tissue and below organ system.

questions.prompt: A group of organs that work together to perform a major life function — circulatory system, digestive system, respiratory system.
questions.explanation: An organ system is a group of organs cooperating to carry out a major life function. The human body has 11 organ systems: integumentary (skin), skeletal, muscular, nervous, endocrine, cardiovascular/circulatory, lymphatic/immune, respiratory, digestive, urinary/excretory, reproductive. Organ systems interact to maintain homeostasis — for example, the respiratory and circulatory systems work together to deliver oxygen to every cell. NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly tests organ-system interactions in homeostasis questions: 'Describe how two organ systems work together to maintain stable internal conditions during exercise' is a classic CRQ-style item.

questions.prompt: The double-helix molecule that stores the genetic instructions for building and operating every living cell.
questions.explanation: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is the molecule of heredity. James Watson and Francis Crick published the double-helix structure in Nature in April 1953, building directly on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography image known as Photo 51. DNA is a polymer of nucleotides; each nucleotide has a deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate group, and one of four nitrogenous bases (A, T, G, C). Bases pair specifically: A with T, G with C — held together by hydrogen bonds. The base sequence is the genetic code. DNA is universal across all known life, which is one of the strongest pieces of evidence for common ancestry. NY Living Environment Regents tests DNA constantly on Part A, Part B, and Part D.

questions.prompt: A segment of DNA that codes for one specific protein (or RNA molecule) — the basic unit of heredity.
questions.explanation: A gene is a DNA segment whose base sequence specifies the amino acid sequence of one protein (or sometimes one functional RNA). Different versions of the same gene are called alleles. The human genome contains roughly 20,000 protein-coding genes, accounting for less than 2% of the DNA in our chromosomes. Mendel's 'factors' (1866) are now known as genes. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses gene diagrams to test the relationship between DNA sequence and protein structure; a single base change (mutation) can change one amino acid and alter protein function — the basis of sickle-cell anemia is one such substitution in the hemoglobin gene.

questions.prompt: A condensed package of DNA wrapped around proteins, visible during cell division — humans have 46 of them.
questions.explanation: A chromosome is a single, very long DNA molecule wound around histone proteins. Humans have 46 chromosomes in body cells (23 pairs) — 22 pairs of autosomes plus one pair of sex chromosomes (XX = female, XY = male). Each parent contributes 23 chromosomes through their gametes (sperm and egg), produced by meiosis. Before cell division, each chromosome is replicated to form two identical sister chromatids joined at a centromere. The NYSED Living Environment Part B-2 routinely uses chromosome diagrams in questions about mitosis, meiosis, karyotypes, and chromosomal disorders such as Down syndrome (an extra copy of chromosome 21).

questions.prompt: The complete set of all the DNA in an organism — every chromosome, every gene, every base pair.
questions.explanation: A genome is the total genetic material of an organism. The human genome contains about 3 billion base pairs distributed across 23 chromosome pairs and roughly 20,000 protein-coding genes. The Human Genome Project (1990-2003) was a 13-year international effort that produced the first complete sequence of the human genome and revolutionized medical genetics, biotechnology, and forensic science. Comparing genomes across species is one of the strongest modern lines of evidence for common ancestry: humans and chimpanzees share about 98.8% of their genome. NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to draw conclusions about evolutionary relatedness from genome comparison tables.

questions.prompt: The single subunit (sugar + phosphate + base) that links together to form DNA or RNA.
questions.explanation: A nucleotide has three parts: a 5-carbon sugar (deoxyribose in DNA, ribose in RNA), a phosphate group, and one nitrogenous base. In DNA the bases are adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine; in RNA, thymine is replaced by uracil. Nucleotides link to form a polymer — the DNA or RNA strand. Base-pairing rules (A-T, G-C in DNA; A-U, G-C in RNA) are central to DNA replication, transcription, and translation. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 commonly shows a DNA strand and asks students to write the complementary strand or the mRNA strand using base-pairing rules, then use a codon chart to translate to amino acids.

questions.prompt: A version of a gene — one parent typically gives one of these, the other parent gives the other.
questions.explanation: An allele is one of the alternative forms of a gene. For a gene with two alleles (often labeled with letters — capital for dominant, lowercase for recessive), an organism has two alleles total (one from each parent). The combination (e.g., TT, Tt, tt) is the genotype. Gregor Mendel, in his pea-plant experiments published in 1866, called these 'factors' and showed they segregate during gamete formation — what we now call meiosis. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses Punnett squares constantly, requiring students to identify parent alleles, predict offspring genotype and phenotype ratios, and explain dominant-recessive patterns.

questions.prompt: An allele whose phenotype is expressed even when only one copy is present (heterozygous) — usually written with a capital letter.
questions.explanation: A dominant allele determines the phenotype when at least one copy is present in the genotype. If T (tall) is dominant over t (short), both TT and Tt plants will be tall — only tt plants will be short. Mendel discovered this 'law of dominance' in 1866 by crossing true-breeding tall and short pea plants and observing that all F1 offspring were tall, then 3:1 tall:short in the F2 generation. NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely uses pea-plant or pet-trait Punnett squares and asks students to predict the phenotype ratio when dominant and recessive alleles segregate.

questions.prompt: An allele whose phenotype only appears when both copies are present (homozygous) — usually written with a lowercase letter.
questions.explanation: A recessive allele only determines the phenotype when both copies are recessive (homozygous recessive: tt, bb, dd). In a heterozygous individual (Tt), the recessive allele is present but masked by the dominant one. Many human genetic disorders are recessive — cystic fibrosis, sickle-cell anemia, Tay-Sachs, PKU — meaning a person can be a heterozygous carrier without showing the disease but can pass the allele to children. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses pedigree charts and Punnett squares to test recessive inheritance, particularly identifying carriers and predicting the probability that two heterozygous parents will produce an affected child (1 in 4).

questions.prompt: The grid-style chart used to predict the genotypes and phenotypes of offspring from a genetic cross.
questions.explanation: The Punnett square, devised by British geneticist Reginald Punnett around 1905, predicts the outcomes of a cross by writing one parent's gamete alleles across the top and the other parent's gamete alleles down the side. Each box represents one offspring genotype. A monohybrid cross (Tt x Tt) gives 1 TT : 2 Tt : 1 tt, with a 3:1 dominant:recessive phenotype ratio. NY Living Environment Regents Part B almost always includes at least one Punnett-square item; students must build the square, record offspring genotypes, count ratios, and translate to expected phenotypes and probabilities.

questions.prompt: The genetic makeup of an organism — the specific pair of alleles (like Tt or BB) for a trait.
questions.explanation: Genotype is the combination of alleles an individual carries for a gene — typically written as two letters (TT, Tt, or tt) since most diploid organisms have two alleles per gene. The genotype is contrasted with the phenotype (the observable trait that results). Homozygous genotypes have two identical alleles (TT or tt); heterozygous have two different alleles (Tt). The same phenotype can result from different genotypes (TT and Tt both look tall). NY Living Environment Regents Part B and Part D use the genotype-phenotype distinction in nearly every inheritance question; students must identify both for parents and offspring in Punnett squares and pedigree analyses.

questions.prompt: The observable trait — the physical or behavioral expression — that results from an organism's genotype.
questions.explanation: Phenotype is the trait you can see or measure — flower color, height, eye color, blood type, presence of a disease. The phenotype is shaped by the genotype, but environment also plays a role. A pea plant with the genotype TT will be tall, but if it grows in poor soil it may not reach its full potential height. Similarly, human skin color depends both on genes and on sun exposure. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses the genotype-phenotype distinction in nearly every inheritance question and especially in environmental-effect-on-phenotype questions, where the same genotype produces different phenotypes in different environments.

questions.prompt: Having two identical alleles for a gene — either two dominant (TT) or two recessive (tt).
questions.explanation: An individual is homozygous when both alleles for a gene are the same: homozygous dominant (TT, BB) or homozygous recessive (tt, bb). Mendel's 'true-breeding' or 'purebred' plants were homozygous — when self-pollinated they produced offspring identical to themselves for that trait. Recessive genetic disorders like cystic fibrosis only appear in homozygous recessive individuals (cc). NY Living Environment Regents Part B regularly uses Punnett squares to predict the percentage of offspring that will be homozygous vs. heterozygous and asks students to identify what genotype combinations produce a homozygous offspring.

questions.prompt: Having two different alleles for a gene — one dominant and one recessive (Tt).
questions.explanation: A heterozygous individual carries two different alleles for a gene (e.g., Tt, Bb). In simple dominance, the dominant allele's phenotype shows. Heterozygous individuals are called carriers for recessive disorders — they don't show the disease but can pass the recessive allele to offspring. Two heterozygous carriers (Aa x Aa) have a 25% chance of producing a homozygous-recessive affected child. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses heterozygous parents in Punnett squares and asks students to compute the probability of carriers vs. affected offspring; pedigrees often require identifying which individuals must be heterozygous given the family pattern.

questions.prompt: An inheritance pattern in which both alleles are expressed equally and visibly in the heterozygous individual — like AB blood type.
questions.explanation: In codominance, neither allele is dominant — both are fully expressed in the heterozygote. The classic example is the ABO blood group: a person with one A allele and one B allele (IAIB) has type AB blood, with both A and B antigens on their red blood cells. Another example is sickle-cell trait — heterozygotes (one normal hemoglobin allele, one sickle allele) produce both types of hemoglobin and are partially resistant to malaria, an example of how a 'harmful' allele can be maintained in a population by selection pressure. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests codominance with multiple-allele systems like blood type.

questions.prompt: An inherited trait carried on a sex chromosome (usually X) — color blindness and hemophilia are classic examples.
questions.explanation: A sex-linked trait is one whose gene is on a sex chromosome — almost always the X chromosome since the Y carries very few genes. Males (XY) have only one copy of any X-linked gene, so a recessive allele on the X is expressed in males with no second copy to mask it. This explains why disorders like red-green color blindness and hemophilia are far more common in males than females; a female (XX) usually has a second normal allele on her other X. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses sex-linked Punnett squares (X^a X x X^A Y) and pedigrees with skipped-generation father-to-grandson patterns to test this concept.

questions.prompt: A change in the DNA base sequence — can be neutral, harmful, or beneficial; the ultimate source of new genetic variation.
questions.explanation: A mutation is any change in a DNA sequence. Point mutations alter a single base (substitution, insertion, or deletion); chromosomal mutations affect larger pieces. Mutations in body cells (somatic) affect the individual but are not inherited; mutations in gametes are passed to offspring. Most mutations are neutral, some are harmful (sickle-cell, cystic fibrosis), and a few are beneficial — providing the raw variation on which natural selection acts. Causes include radiation, chemical mutagens (tobacco smoke), viruses, and random copying errors during DNA replication. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses mutation in evolution and in genetic disorder questions, and Part A asks for the definition.

questions.prompt: A modern biotechnology technique using CRISPR-Cas9 to precisely cut and alter specific DNA sequences in a cell.
questions.explanation: Gene editing alters specific DNA sequences in living cells with great precision. The most common modern tool is CRISPR-Cas9, developed by Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier, who won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2020. A guide RNA directs the Cas9 enzyme to a specific DNA sequence, which is then cut; the cell repairs the cut, often disrupting or inserting a new sequence. Medical uses include treating sickle-cell disease and inherited blindness. NY Living Environment Regents Part B includes biotechnology questions on CRISPR and other gene-editing technologies, often asking students to identify benefits (treating disease) and risks (off-target effects, ethical concerns about germline editing).

questions.prompt: A photograph of all an individual's chromosomes arranged in pairs — used to diagnose chromosomal disorders like Down syndrome.
questions.explanation: A karyotype is an organized photograph showing all of an individual's chromosomes, paired and arranged by size. Cells are arrested in metaphase (when chromosomes are most condensed), stained, photographed, and the chromosomes are cut out and paired. A normal human karyotype has 46 chromosomes (23 pairs); Down syndrome shows trisomy 21 (three copies of chromosome 21) due to nondisjunction during meiosis. Karyotypes also reveal sex (XX or XY) and other chromosomal abnormalities. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses karyotype images and asks students to identify the disorder or sex shown.

questions.prompt: The deliberate modification of an organism's DNA — including inserting genes from one species into another, as in insulin-producing bacteria.
questions.explanation: Genetic engineering directly alters an organism's DNA, often by inserting genes from another species. A landmark example is the production of human insulin in E. coli bacteria — the human insulin gene is inserted into a bacterial plasmid, the bacteria are grown in fermentation tanks, and the insulin is purified for treating diabetes. Other applications include Bt corn (carrying a bacterial toxin gene to repel insects), golden rice (engineered to produce vitamin A), and gene-therapy treatments. NY Living Environment Regents Part B includes biotechnology questions where students evaluate benefits, risks, and ethical concerns of genetic engineering.

questions.prompt: Choosing which individuals to breed based on desired traits — the technique humans have used for ten thousand years to develop dogs and crops.
questions.explanation: Selective breeding (artificial selection) is the deliberate mating of organisms with desired heritable traits — generation after generation — to amplify those traits in future generations. Humans have used selective breeding for at least 10,000 years to develop modern wheat, corn, rice, every dog breed, dairy cattle, and laboratory mice. Darwin used the parallel with artificial selection to introduce natural selection in On the Origin of Species (1859). NY Living Environment Regents Part B contrasts selective breeding (intentional, generations-long) with genetic engineering (precise, one-step DNA change) and with natural selection (no human involvement, driven by environmental fitness).

questions.prompt: A family-tree diagram tracking the inheritance of a trait across generations, using circles for females and squares for males.
questions.explanation: A pedigree chart uses standard symbols — circles for females, squares for males, filled symbols for affected individuals, horizontal lines for mating couples, vertical lines descending to offspring — to map a trait's inheritance through a family across generations. Pedigrees are used to identify whether a trait is dominant or recessive, autosomal or sex-linked, and to predict the likelihood that future children will be affected. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 commonly shows a multi-generation pedigree and asks students to determine the inheritance pattern (autosomal recessive disorders skip generations; X-linked patterns affect mostly males).

questions.prompt: A heterozygous individual who shows no symptoms of a recessive disorder but can pass the recessive allele to their children.
questions.explanation: A carrier is heterozygous for a recessive allele — they have one normal allele and one recessive disease allele. They do not show the disease (the dominant normal allele masks it) but can pass the recessive allele to offspring. If two carriers (Aa x Aa) have children, the predicted ratio is 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa, so 25% of offspring will be affected and 50% will be carriers. Cystic fibrosis, sickle-cell anemia, Tay-Sachs, and PKU all show this pattern. NY Living Environment Regents Part B pedigree questions often require identifying carriers among phenotypically normal family members, particularly when an affected child is born to two unaffected parents.

questions.prompt: The mathematical chance — usually expressed as a fraction or percentage — that a particular genetic outcome will occur.
questions.explanation: Probability is the likelihood of an event, expressed from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), or as a percentage. In genetics, Punnett squares predict probabilities of offspring genotypes and phenotypes: a Tt x Tt cross gives 3/4 (75%) tall and 1/4 (25%) short. Probabilities are statistical predictions — actual offspring numbers can differ from expectations, especially with small sample sizes (a single Tt x Tt cross might produce 4 tall and 0 short or vice versa). The probability of each independent event in a sequence multiplies (e.g., 1/4 x 1/4 = 1/16 for two affected children). NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely tests this.

questions.prompt: Crossing an organism showing the dominant phenotype with a homozygous recessive partner to find out if it is TT or Tt.
questions.explanation: A test cross determines whether an organism showing the dominant phenotype is homozygous dominant (TT) or heterozygous (Tt). The mystery individual is crossed with a homozygous recessive (tt). If all offspring show the dominant trait, the mystery parent was TT. If any offspring show the recessive trait, the mystery parent was Tt. Mendel used test crosses in his original 1866 pea-plant experiments to verify his hypothesis about how factors (alleles) segregate. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses test-cross questions especially in agricultural breeding scenarios where farmers need to know whether a champion animal will breed true.

questions.prompt: Medical service that helps families with a history of inherited disease understand their genetic risks before having children.
questions.explanation: Genetic counseling combines pedigree analysis, genetic testing, and probability calculations to help families understand their risk of passing on heritable disorders. A genetic counselor builds a family pedigree, may order DNA tests to detect carrier status (for cystic fibrosis, Tay-Sachs, BRCA breast-cancer alleles, etc.), and discusses options including prenatal testing, IVF with embryo screening, and reproductive decisions. NY Living Environment Regents Part B presents scenarios where prospective parents from a family with a recessive disorder must compute the probability that their child will be a carrier or affected and discusses the role of genetic counseling.

questions.prompt: Cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells from one parent cell — used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction.
questions.explanation: Mitosis is nuclear division producing two daughter cells genetically identical to the parent. It proceeds through four visible stages: prophase (chromosomes condense), metaphase (chromosomes line up at the equator), anaphase (sister chromatids separate to opposite poles), telophase (nuclear envelopes reform). Mitosis is used for growth (a zygote becoming an embryo and then an adult), repair (healing a cut), and asexual reproduction in many organisms. Each daughter cell receives a full set of chromosomes (46 in humans). NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses mitosis diagrams and asks students to identify the stages and explain why daughter cells are genetically identical to the parent.

questions.prompt: Cell division that produces four genetically unique haploid cells — used only to make gametes (sperm and eggs).
questions.explanation: Meiosis is a special two-stage cell division that produces gametes — sperm and eggs — each with half the parent's chromosome number (haploid). In humans, meiosis turns a diploid cell with 46 chromosomes into four haploid cells with 23 chromosomes each. Two key sources of genetic variation occur during meiosis: crossing over in prophase I (homologous chromosomes exchange segments) and independent assortment in metaphase I (each pair lines up randomly). These shuffling events plus random fertilization produce nearly limitless genetic variation in offspring. NY Living Environment Regents Part B contrasts mitosis (identical, body cells) with meiosis (variable, gametes).

questions.prompt: One of two identical copies of a chromosome, joined at the centromere right after DNA replication.
questions.explanation: After DNA replication during S-phase (before mitosis or meiosis), each chromosome consists of two identical copies called sister chromatids, joined at a structure called the centromere. During anaphase of mitosis (and anaphase II of meiosis), the centromere splits and sister chromatids are pulled to opposite poles by spindle fibers, becoming separate daughter chromosomes. In meiosis I, homologous chromosomes separate without splitting the chromatids; in meiosis II, sister chromatids separate as in mitosis. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses chromatid diagrams to test understanding of when DNA replicates and when sister chromatids physically separate.

questions.prompt: A microtubule structure that attaches to chromosomes during cell division and pulls them to opposite poles.
questions.explanation: Spindle fibers are bundles of microtubules — protein cables — that form during prophase and attach to chromosomes at structures called kinetochores located at the centromere. During metaphase, spindle fibers from opposite poles line chromosomes up at the cell's equator. During anaphase, the fibers shorten, pulling sister chromatids to opposite poles. The anti-cancer drug Taxol works by stabilizing spindle fibers so they cannot shorten, halting cancer cell division. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses spindle-fiber diagrams to test the mechanics of chromosome separation in both mitosis and meiosis.

questions.prompt: The final stage of cell division, when the cytoplasm physically pinches apart to form two daughter cells.
questions.explanation: Cytokinesis is the splitting of the cytoplasm following mitosis or meiosis to produce two physically separate daughter cells. Animal cells pinch in the middle as a contractile ring of actin filaments tightens — forming a cleavage furrow. Plant cells, which have rigid cell walls, build a new cell plate at the center that develops into a new cell wall, dividing the parent in two. Cytokinesis usually overlaps with the end of telophase. Without cytokinesis, mitosis would produce a single cell with two nuclei. NY Living Environment Regents Part B may show photographs or diagrams of dividing cells and ask students to distinguish between mitosis (nuclear division) and cytokinesis (cell-body division).

questions.prompt: A sex cell — a sperm or an egg — containing half the normal chromosome number; formed by meiosis.
questions.explanation: A gamete is a haploid sex cell — sperm or egg — produced by meiosis from a diploid germ-line cell. Human gametes carry 23 chromosomes, half the body-cell number (46). When a sperm fertilizes an egg, the diploid chromosome number is restored in the zygote. Male gametes (sperm, spermatozoa) are produced in the testes and are small, flagellated, and produced continuously in millions per day. Female gametes (eggs, ova) are produced in the ovaries, are much larger (contain yolk and cytoplasm), and one is released per menstrual cycle. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses gamete formation to test the connection between meiosis and sexual reproduction.

questions.prompt: The fusion of a sperm and egg to form a single cell with a full set of chromosomes.
questions.explanation: Fertilization is the union of two haploid gametes (sperm + egg) to produce one diploid zygote — restoring the full chromosome number (46 in humans). Internal fertilization happens inside the female body (most mammals, reptiles, birds); external fertilization happens in water (most fish, amphibians). Fertilization combines two unique sets of alleles, producing offspring with genetic variation distinct from either parent. Random fertilization — any of millions of sperm meeting any of many eggs — is one of three main sources of genetic variation in sexually reproducing populations, alongside meiotic crossing over and independent assortment. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests this.

questions.prompt: The single fertilized cell formed when sperm meets egg — the starting point of every new individual.
questions.explanation: A zygote is the single diploid cell formed when a sperm fertilizes an egg. It contains 46 chromosomes — 23 from each parent — and a unique genetic combination different from either parent. The zygote begins mitotic divisions within hours; after about 5 days it forms a blastocyst, then implants in the uterine lining. The zygote contains all the genetic information needed to develop into a complete organism. NY Living Environment Regents Part A defines zygote; Part B tests the sequence: gametes -> fertilization -> zygote -> embryo -> fetus -> newborn, and which steps involve mitosis vs. meiosis.

questions.prompt: The developing offspring from about 2 weeks to 8 weeks after fertilization — the stage during which all major organs first form.
questions.explanation: An embryo is a developing organism between fertilization and the start of the recognizable fetal stage. In humans, the embryonic period spans roughly the first 8 weeks; during this time, the blastocyst implants in the uterine wall, gastrulation produces three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm), and organogenesis builds the heart, brain, spinal cord, limbs, and digestive tract. The embryo is most vulnerable to teratogens during this period. NY Living Environment Regents Part B includes embryo questions in reproduction-and-development units and uses embryology (similarities across vertebrate embryos) as evidence of common ancestry.

questions.prompt: The temporary organ that connects the developing fetus to the mother's uterus, exchanging nutrients, oxygen, and wastes through the umbilical cord.
questions.explanation: The placenta is a disc-shaped organ that develops on the wall of the uterus during pregnancy. The fetus is connected to it by the umbilical cord. Fetal and maternal blood do not mix directly — instead, the placenta provides a thin barrier across which nutrients and oxygen diffuse from the mother to the fetus, and CO2 and metabolic wastes diffuse the other way. The placenta also produces hormones (progesterone, hCG) that maintain pregnancy. Unfortunately, some substances also cross the placenta — including alcohol, nicotine, many drugs, and infectious agents — which is why prenatal care emphasizes avoiding teratogens. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests placental function in pregnancy diagrams.

questions.prompt: The process by which unspecialized cells in an embryo become specialized cells like nerve, muscle, or skin.
questions.explanation: Differentiation is the process by which initially identical embryonic cells become specialized — turning on different sets of genes to become muscle, nerve, blood, bone, leaf, or root cells. Every cell in the body carries the same complete DNA, but each cell type expresses only the genes it needs. Chemical signals from neighboring cells, position in the embryo, and timing all influence which genes turn on. Differentiation in mammals is largely irreversible (a fully differentiated skin cell cannot normally become a neuron) — which is why stem cells are valuable. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests differentiation in connection with stem-cell biology and gene expression.

questions.prompt: The shaping of an embryo's overall body plan and the formation of its tissues, organs, and limbs.
questions.explanation: Morphogenesis (Greek for 'shape-formation') is the developmental process by which an embryo acquires its three-dimensional body plan — head versus tail, dorsal versus ventral, left versus right — and its organs take their characteristic shape. It involves coordinated cell migration, programmed cell death (apoptosis — for example, the cells between embryonic fingers die to separate them), changes in cell adhesion, and tissue folding. Hox genes provide positional information along the head-to-tail axis. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses developmental biology as a connection between cell biology, genetics, and evolution — embryological similarity across vertebrates is classic evidence of common ancestry.

questions.prompt: A specialized microenvironment in adult tissues that maintains stem cells and regulates when they divide.
questions.explanation: A stem cell niche is the local tissue microenvironment that maintains a population of adult stem cells — including the specific neighboring cells, signaling molecules, and extracellular matrix that keep stem cells from differentiating until needed. Bone marrow contains niches for hematopoietic stem cells (which make all blood cell types); the base of intestinal crypts holds niches for the stem cells that constantly replace intestinal lining; hair follicles, the brain, and skin all have stem-cell niches. Understanding niches is central to regenerative medicine. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses stem-cell biology when testing tissue repair, biotechnology applications, and the ethics of stem-cell research.

questions.prompt: The embryonic period when major organs — heart, brain, liver, kidneys — first form from the three germ layers.
questions.explanation: Organogenesis is the embryonic stage when the three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) give rise to all the major organs. In humans this occurs roughly between weeks 3 and 8 of pregnancy. The ectoderm forms skin and the nervous system; the mesoderm forms muscle, bone, heart, kidneys, and blood; the endoderm forms the lining of the digestive and respiratory tracts. Disruption during this critical period — by alcohol, certain drugs, infections like rubella, or radiation — can produce major birth defects. NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely tests the importance of prenatal care during organogenesis when the embryo is most vulnerable to teratogens.

questions.prompt: A chemical message (hormone, growth factor, or local signal) that triggers a specific developmental response in target cells.
questions.explanation: Developmental signals are chemical messages that coordinate when and where embryonic cells divide, migrate, differentiate, or die. Examples include morphogens (signaling molecules that form concentration gradients across the embryo and tell cells their position), hormones (estrogen, testosterone driving sexual differentiation), and growth factors (chemicals that stimulate cell proliferation). The same signaling molecules are reused throughout development and adult life, which is one reason that disrupting them with hormone-mimicking chemicals (endocrine disruptors) can have wide-ranging effects. NY Living Environment Regents Part B includes signaling questions in homeostasis and hormonal regulation discussions.

questions.prompt: Reproduction with one parent producing offspring genetically identical to itself — used by bacteria, hydras, and many plants.
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction uses mitosis to produce offspring that are genetic copies (clones) of the single parent. It is fast, energy-efficient, and requires no mate — a major advantage when conditions are stable. Examples include binary fission in bacteria, budding in hydra and yeast, regeneration in starfish, and vegetative propagation in strawberries (runners) or potatoes (tubers). The big disadvantage: no genetic variation. If the environment changes, an entire clonal population may be wiped out by a single disease. NY Living Environment Regents Part B contrasts asexual reproduction (rapid, no variation) with sexual reproduction (slow, high variation) and asks students to predict which is advantageous in different environments.

questions.prompt: Reproduction with two parents, each contributing one gamete; produces offspring with unique combinations of genes.
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction combines genetic material from two parents through meiosis (gamete formation) and fertilization. The resulting offspring carry unique combinations of alleles never seen in either parent. Three sources of variation occur: crossing over during meiosis I, independent assortment of homologous chromosomes, and random fertilization. The variation produced by sexual reproduction is the raw material on which natural selection acts; populations that reproduce sexually can adapt to changing conditions much more rapidly than asexual populations. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests this in evolution questions about why sexual reproduction is widespread despite its costs (time, energy, finding a mate).

questions.prompt: An organism genetically identical to its parent — produced naturally by asexual reproduction or artificially through cell biotechnology.
questions.explanation: A clone is an organism (or cell) genetically identical to its parent. Clones occur naturally in asexual reproduction — bacteria dividing by binary fission, plant runners producing new strawberry plants, and even identical twins (formed when a single zygote splits in two). Artificial cloning includes plant cuttings, somatic cell nuclear transfer (the technique used to clone Dolly the sheep in 1996 by Ian Wilmut at the Roslin Institute), and the cloning of crop varieties. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses cloning examples in biotechnology questions, often asking students to evaluate the scientific benefits, risks, and ethical concerns.

questions.prompt: Asexual reproduction in which a new organism grows out as a small bump from the parent, then breaks off — used by yeast and hydra.
questions.explanation: Budding is a form of asexual reproduction in which a new organism develops as an outgrowth (a bud) of the parent. The parent's cells mitotically divide to form the bud, which eventually separates as a genetically identical individual. Yeast (Saccharomyces) bud microscopically and rapidly. Hydra (a small freshwater cnidarian) buds visibly under a microscope, a favorite classroom demonstration. Some plants (kalanchoe, ginger) bud at leaf margins or rhizomes. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses budding diagrams and asks students to identify it as a form of asexual reproduction and to predict the genetic relationship between parent and offspring (identical).

questions.prompt: Re-growing lost body parts — like a starfish growing a new arm or a salamander growing a new tail.
questions.explanation: Regeneration is the regrowth of lost or damaged body parts. Some animals can regenerate entire limbs or whole bodies (planaria flatworms cut into pieces will each grow into a complete worm; salamanders regrow limbs and tails; starfish regrow arms — and sometimes a single starfish arm with part of the central disc can regrow the whole starfish). In humans, regeneration is more limited: skin heals, liver tissue can regrow substantially, but most other tissues form scar tissue rather than replace lost cells. Stem-cell biology aims to improve human regenerative capacity. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses regeneration as an example of asexual reproduction in some species and tissue repair in others.

questions.prompt: Medical care a pregnant person receives during pregnancy to support healthy fetal development.
questions.explanation: Prenatal care is the medical care a pregnant person receives during pregnancy — including nutrition (folic acid to prevent neural-tube defects), screening tests (ultrasound, amniocentesis to detect chromosomal disorders), prenatal vitamins, and avoidance of teratogens. Adequate prenatal care reduces the risk of premature birth, low birth weight, and birth defects. The first eight weeks (during organogenesis) are the most critical, since most major organs form then and the developing embryo is most vulnerable to alcohol, drugs, infection, and radiation. NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely tests prenatal-care concepts as part of human-development units and asks students to explain why early pregnancy is the riskiest period.

questions.prompt: Any substance (alcohol, drugs, certain infections, radiation) that can disrupt fetal development and cause birth defects.
questions.explanation: A teratogen is an environmental agent — chemical, biological, or physical — that can cause birth defects when a fetus is exposed during development. Classic examples: alcohol (causes fetal alcohol syndrome with characteristic facial features and developmental delays), thalidomide (a sedative that caused severe limb malformations in the 1960s), rubella virus, the Zika virus (causes microcephaly), tobacco smoke (low birth weight, preterm birth), and ionizing radiation. Teratogens cross the placenta from mother to fetus. Most are most harmful during the first trimester when organs are forming. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses teratogens in human development and in environmental-health questions.

questions.prompt: A chemical message produced by a gland and carried by the bloodstream to target cells — coordinates puberty, pregnancy, and the menstrual cycle.
questions.explanation: Hormones are chemical messengers produced by endocrine glands (pituitary, thyroid, adrenals, pancreas, ovaries, testes) and transported through the bloodstream to target cells with matching receptors. Reproductive hormones are central in this unit: FSH and LH from the pituitary stimulate the gonads; estrogen and progesterone from the ovaries regulate the menstrual cycle and prepare the uterus for pregnancy; testosterone from the testes drives male puberty; hCG from the placenta maintains pregnancy.
