NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests hormonal regulation in negative-feedback diagrams of the menstrual cycle and asks students to explain how birth-control pills disrupt the natural cycle.

questions.prompt: The 28-day hormonal cycle that prepares the female reproductive system for possible pregnancy each month.
questions.explanation: The menstrual cycle is roughly 28 days long, regulated by an interaction between the hypothalamus, pituitary, and ovaries. The ovarian cycle has three phases: follicular (FSH causes a follicle to mature), ovulation around day 14 (LH surge releases an egg), and luteal (progesterone from the corpus luteum prepares the uterine lining). The uterine cycle parallels this: the endometrium thickens, then either supports an embryo or sheds (menstruation, days 1-5). If fertilization occurs, hCG from the embryo maintains the corpus luteum and progesterone keeps the lining intact. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses menstrual-cycle diagrams in human reproduction questions.

questions.prompt: Medical methods (hormonal, barrier, or surgical) used to prevent pregnancy by interfering with ovulation, fertilization, or implantation.
questions.explanation: Birth control methods prevent pregnancy by interrupting one of three steps: ovulation, fertilization, or implantation. Hormonal methods (the pill, patch, ring, injection, implant) use synthetic estrogen and progesterone to suppress FSH and LH, preventing ovulation. Barrier methods (condoms, diaphragms) physically block sperm. IUDs work by preventing fertilization or implantation. Surgical methods (vasectomy, tubal ligation) provide permanent contraception. Each method has different effectiveness and side effects. NY Living Environment Regents Part A may name birth-control methods; Part B uses hormonal contraception in feedback-loop questions about how negative feedback from synthetic hormones suppresses the hypothalamus and pituitary.

questions.prompt: Darwin's mechanism for evolution — the process where individuals with favorable inherited traits survive, reproduce, and pass those traits on at higher rates than others.
questions.explanation: Natural selection is the central mechanism of evolution proposed by Charles Darwin in On the Origin of Species (1859) and independently by Alfred Russel Wallace. Its four conditions: (1) populations show heritable variation; (2) populations produce more offspring than can survive; (3) competition for limited resources occurs; (4) individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and reproduce more — passing those alleles to the next generation. Over many generations, the population's allele frequencies shift. Classic evidence: Darwin's Galapagos finches with different beak shapes for different food sources, peppered moths during the Industrial Revolution, and antibiotic resistance in bacteria. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests natural selection in every evolution unit.

questions.prompt: An organism's success at surviving and reproducing in its environment — measured by how many offspring it leaves.
questions.explanation: In evolutionary biology, fitness is NOT about strength or speed — it is about how many surviving, fertile offspring an individual produces. A peacock with elaborate feathers may be less athletic but if his display attracts more mates, he has higher fitness. A bacterium with antibiotic resistance has higher fitness in an environment with antibiotics. Fitness is always relative to the environment: a fitness trait in one environment can be neutral or harmful in another (thick fur is fit in the Arctic, harmful in the desert). NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests this misconception constantly — students must recognize that fitness equals reproductive success, not just survival or strength.

questions.prompt: The fact that most species produce more offspring than can possibly survive — sets the stage for the struggle for existence Darwin described.
questions.explanation: Overproduction means species produce many more offspring than the environment can support. A single oak tree may produce thousands of acorns each year; a frog may lay thousands of eggs; even slow-reproducing humans produce far more than the Earth could sustain at maximum biological rate. Most offspring will die before reproducing — from predation, starvation, disease, or random misfortune. This sets up competition and selection. Darwin took this idea from Thomas Malthus's 1798 essay on human population, applying it to all life. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses overproduction as the first step in any natural-selection explanation; students must mention it when explaining how selection happens.

questions.prompt: The struggle among organisms for limited resources (food, water, mates, space) — drives natural selection.
questions.explanation: Competition for limited resources — food, water, mates, light, space, nesting sites — is the engine that turns variation into selection. Intraspecific competition (within a species) is especially intense because individuals need exactly the same resources. Interspecific competition (between species) can drive species to evolve different niches (resource partitioning). In Darwin's Galapagos finches, multiple species coexist on the same island by specializing on different food sources, reducing competition. NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to identify the resource being competed for in a scenario and predict which variants will be favored as a result.

questions.prompt: The pattern in which some individuals in a population survive and reproduce more than others because their inherited traits give them an advantage.
questions.explanation: Differential survival is the outcome of natural selection: individuals with favorable heritable traits survive at higher rates and have more offspring than individuals without those traits. Over many generations, the favorable traits become more common in the population — that is evolution. In the Beaks of Finches lab (a NYSED required Living Environment lab), students use different tools (chopsticks, spoons, tweezers) as 'beaks' to pick up different 'seeds' in a fixed time, simulating differential feeding success — and therefore differential survival and reproduction. NY Living Environment Regents Part D tests this with lab data from the Beaks of Finches activity.

questions.prompt: The preserved remains and traces of organisms in rock — gives a chronological record of life forms across Earth's history.
questions.explanation: The fossil record preserves the history of life as fossils in sedimentary rock layers. Older fossils lie deeper; younger fossils lie closer to the surface (the law of superposition). Index fossils — common, widespread, short-lived species — are used to date rock layers across continents. Transitional fossils like Archaeopteryx (between reptiles and birds, found 1861), Tiktaalik (between fish and tetrapods, found 2004), and the Australopithecus afarensis specimen 'Lucy' (between earlier apes and modern humans, found 1974) document major evolutionary transitions. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests fossil-record reasoning when given a stratigraphic column and asked which fossils share more recent common ancestors.

questions.prompt: The comparison of body structures across species to identify homologies — same bones, sometimes different functions.
questions.explanation: Comparative anatomy compares the body structures of different species. Homologous structures (same underlying anatomy, different functions — human arm, whale flipper, bat wing, cat leg) reveal descent from a common ancestor. Analogous structures (different anatomy, same function — bird wing and insect wing) reveal convergent evolution in similar environments. Vestigial structures (the human appendix, whale pelvic bones, blind cavefish eye sockets) are reduced or non-functional remnants of features useful in ancestors. Darwin used comparative anatomy as a centerpiece of his evidence in On the Origin of Species (1859). NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses limb diagrams to test this constantly.

questions.prompt: The strongest modern evidence for evolutionary relationships — comparing the order of DNA bases or amino acid sequences across species.
questions.explanation: Molecular evidence — comparing DNA sequences, protein amino-acid sequences, or other biological molecules — is the most powerful modern tool for establishing evolutionary relationships. Closer DNA-sequence similarity = more recent common ancestor. Humans and chimpanzees share ~98.8% of their DNA; humans and mice share ~85%; humans and bananas share roughly 60%. The cytochrome c protein, used in cellular respiration, is so ancient that it's compared across all eukaryotes. The NYSED Relationships and Biodiversity lab uses simulated molecular evidence to determine relationships among plant species. NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely tests molecular evidence with sequence-comparison tables.

questions.prompt: The study of embryonic development across species — vertebrate embryos look strikingly similar in early stages, evidence of common ancestry.
questions.explanation: Embryology compares the development of different species. Vertebrate embryos — fish, salamander, turtle, chicken, pig, human — pass through strikingly similar early stages, all developing pharyngeal pouches (gill-like openings) and tails. These shared developmental patterns reflect inheritance from a common ancestor. Karl Ernst von Baer described this in 1828; Darwin used it as evidence in On the Origin of Species. Modern evolutionary developmental biology (evo-devo) explores how changes in regulatory genes (like the Hox genes) during development can produce major morphological changes — for example, why bats have such long fingers but the rest of the bones are like other mammals'. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests embryological evidence.

questions.prompt: The geographic distribution of species across the planet — Darwin's Galapagos finches and Australia's marsupials are classic examples.
questions.explanation: Biogeography is the study of where species live and why. Closely related species are often clustered in nearby geographic regions because they share recent common ancestry. Darwin's Galapagos finches (HMS Beagle voyage 1831-1836) were all closely related to one mainland South American finch, but each Galapagos island had its own variant adapted to that island's food. Australia's diverse marsupials (kangaroos, koalas, wombats, Tasmanian devils) evolved in isolation after Australia separated from the other continents. Convergent evolution explains why marsupial mammals and placental mammals from other continents independently evolved similar body forms (marsupial mole vs. true mole). NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests biogeography in evolution questions.

questions.prompt: The total of all alleles present in all individuals of a population — the genetic 'inventory' on which evolution acts.
questions.explanation: A gene pool is the complete set of alleles (versions of all the genes) present in a population at one time. Evolution occurs when the frequencies of alleles in the gene pool change from one generation to the next. The size and composition of the gene pool determine the population's evolutionary potential — a large, diverse gene pool can respond to environmental change; a small, homogeneous gene pool is vulnerable. The mechanisms that change allele frequencies in the gene pool are: natural selection, mutation, gene flow (migration), genetic drift (random change), and non-random mating. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests changes in allele frequency as the definition of microevolution.

questions.prompt: Random change in allele frequencies from one generation to the next — has the biggest effect in small populations.
questions.explanation: Genetic drift is random fluctuation in allele frequencies from generation to generation, caused by chance — which alleles end up in the gametes that successfully fertilize, which individuals happen to survive bad weather, etc. Drift has its largest effect in small populations: in a population of 10 individuals, losing 2 by random chance might eliminate an entire allele. In large populations, random changes average out. Drift is one of the main forces (along with natural selection) that change allele frequencies over generations. The bottleneck effect and founder effect are special cases of drift. NY Living Environment Regents Part B contrasts drift (random) with natural selection (directional).

questions.prompt: A sudden drastic reduction in population size — say a disaster — that leaves only a small, possibly atypical subset of the original gene pool.
questions.explanation: The bottleneck effect is a case of genetic drift in which a population is drastically reduced — by disaster, hunting, habitat destruction, or disease — leaving a small group of survivors whose alleles may not represent the original population. The Northern Elephant Seal, hunted to near-extinction in the 19th century to ~20 individuals, recovered to over 100,000 today, but the modern population shows almost no genetic variation — making it vulnerable to disease. The cheetah went through a bottleneck during the last Ice Age and remains genetically uniform. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses bottleneck examples in conservation biology questions.

questions.prompt: When a few individuals leave the main group to start a new population, carrying only a non-representative slice of the original gene pool.
questions.explanation: The founder effect is another case of genetic drift: a small group leaves the main population to colonize new territory, carrying with them a sample of alleles that may not represent the original gene pool's diversity. Rare alleles may become common in the new population just because they happened to be present in the founders. The Amish population in Pennsylvania, founded by ~200 immigrants in the 18th century, shows elevated frequencies of certain recessive genetic disorders. The diversity of finch species in the Galapagos may have started with one storm-driven flock arriving from the mainland. NY Living Environment Regents Part B contrasts founder effect with bottleneck effect.

questions.prompt: The process by which one species splits into two or more separate species — usually requires populations to be geographically separated for many generations.
questions.explanation: Speciation is the splitting of one ancestral species into two or more new species, no longer capable of interbreeding. The most common mechanism is allopatric speciation: a geographic barrier (a new river, a mountain range, a sea-level change isolating an island) splits a population in two. Each subpopulation accumulates different mutations and adapts to its own environment over many generations. Eventually, when they meet again, they no longer mate or produce fertile offspring — they are separate species. Darwin's Galapagos finches diverged into 14+ species through allopatric speciation on different islands. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses speciation in macroevolution and biodiversity questions.

questions.prompt: The rapid evolution of many species from a single ancestor, each adapted to a different niche — like Darwin's finches and Hawaiian honeycreepers.
questions.explanation: Adaptive radiation is the rapid evolution of many new species from a single ancestral species, each adapted to a distinct ecological niche. The classic example is Darwin's Galapagos finches — at least 14 species evolved from a single mainland ancestor, each with a beak shape suited to a specific food source (small seeds, large seeds, insects, cactus pulp, even blood-drinking on seabird chicks). Other examples include Hawaiian honeycreepers, Australian marsupials, and the explosion of mammal species after the dinosaurs went extinct ~66 million years ago, when ecological opportunities opened up. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests adaptive radiation as a pattern of biodiversity.

questions.prompt: The complete disappearance of a species from Earth — passenger pigeons, mammoths, and the dodo are gone forever.
questions.explanation: Extinction is the permanent loss of a species — no living member left. Earth has seen five major mass extinctions in its history (the largest, the Permian-Triassic ~252 million years ago, wiped out ~96% of marine species; the Cretaceous-Paleogene ~66 million years ago, killed the non-avian dinosaurs). Scientists argue we are now in a sixth mass extinction driven by human activity — habitat loss, pollution, climate change, and overharvesting. Modern extinctions include the dodo (Mauritius, 1681), the passenger pigeon (North America, 1914), the Tasmanian tiger (Australia, 1936), and many more. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses extinction in evolution and conservation questions.

questions.prompt: An organism's role in an ecosystem — what it eats, what eats it, where it lives, and how it interacts with other species.
questions.explanation: A niche (pronounced 'nitch' or 'neesh') is the full ecological role of a species — its habitat, food sources, predators, time of activity (day vs. night), and interactions with other species. Two species cannot occupy exactly the same niche in the same place for long — the competitive exclusion principle predicts that one will outcompete the other or one must evolve a slightly different niche. Resource partitioning is how multiple species coexist by specializing on slightly different resources (Darwin's finches eating different seed sizes; warbler species feeding at different heights in the same tree). NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests niches in ecology and evolution units.

questions.prompt: A species found in only one geographic region and nowhere else on Earth — like the Galapagos tortoise or the Australian platypus.
questions.explanation: An endemic species lives in one specific geographic region — an island, mountain range, lake, or country — and nowhere else. Endemism arises through long isolation, adaptive radiation, and speciation. The Galapagos Islands have many endemic species — the Galapagos tortoise, marine iguana, flightless cormorant. Madagascar has the world's lemurs (95+ endemic species). Endemic species are especially vulnerable to extinction because they have nowhere else to go if their single home is destroyed. NY Living Environment Regents Part B includes endemic species in biodiversity and conservation discussions, often in the context of habitat loss on islands.

questions.prompt: A branching diagram showing inferred evolutionary relationships and common ancestors — built from molecular and anatomical evidence.
questions.explanation: A phylogenetic tree is a branching diagram showing the inferred evolutionary relationships among species. The tips of the branches are the species being compared; the internal branch points (nodes) represent common ancestors. Two species are more closely related when their nodes are more recent. Phylogenetic trees are built from morphological and especially molecular data (DNA sequences, protein sequences). The Tree of Life has three main domains — Bacteria, Archaea, Eukarya — proposed by Carl Woese in 1977 based on ribosomal RNA comparisons. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests phylogenetic-tree (and cladogram) reading: which two species share the most recent common ancestor, and how many ancestral changes separate two given species.

questions.prompt: A trait obtained during an individual's lifetime — like a muscular bicep from weightlifting — that is NOT inherited; the Lamarckian misconception.
questions.explanation: An acquired trait is a feature an individual gains during its lifetime through use, learning, or environment — bigger biceps from weight training, a tan from sun exposure, a scar from injury, knowledge from school. Acquired traits are NOT inherited by offspring because they are not encoded in the gametic DNA. This is a key Regents misconception trap — Jean-Baptiste Lamarck's discredited 'inheritance of acquired characteristics' theory (1809) held that a giraffe stretching its neck reaching for leaves would pass on the longer neck to its offspring. The correct Darwinian explanation: giraffes with variation in neck length already existed; longer-necked variants survived and reproduced more. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests this every year.

questions.prompt: The reason populations have differences among individuals — mutation, sexual reproduction's shuffling, and random fertilization.
questions.explanation: Sources of genetic variation: (1) Mutation — random changes in DNA sequence, the ultimate source of new alleles; (2) Sexual reproduction with crossing over in prophase I of meiosis, where homologous chromosomes exchange segments and create new allele combinations; (3) Independent assortment in metaphase I of meiosis, where each homologous pair lines up randomly; (4) Random fertilization, where any of millions of sperm can fertilize any egg. Variation is the raw material on which natural selection acts — without variation, no evolution. NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely asks students to identify a source of variation in a Punnett-square or pedigree problem.

questions.prompt: Any factor in the environment — predators, climate, food shortage, disease — that favors some heritable traits over others.
questions.explanation: An environmental pressure (also called selective pressure or selection pressure) is any factor in the environment that affects an organism's survival and reproduction differently depending on its inherited traits. Examples: a drought favors plants with deep roots; a new predator favors prey that are well-camouflaged; antibiotic use favors resistant bacteria; cold weather favors warm-blooded animals with thick fur or feathers. When the environmental pressure changes, the direction of natural selection changes — peppered moths during the Industrial Revolution evolved darker coloration when tree bark was stained with soot. NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to identify the environmental pressure in a scenario and predict which variants will be favored.

questions.prompt: A heritable trait that boosts an organism's ability to survive and reproduce in its environment — like the camouflage of a moth.
questions.explanation: A survival advantage is any heritable trait that helps an organism survive and reproduce better than other individuals in the same environment. Camouflage in moths, prey-detection in predators, drought tolerance in plants, antibiotic resistance in bacteria, sickle-cell heterozygote advantage in malaria-endemic regions — all examples. Survival advantages are relative to the environment: the same trait can be advantageous in one environment and harmful in another. Over generations, traits with survival advantages become more common in the population through natural selection. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses 'survival advantage' as the answer to questions about why a particular variant is favored — students must explicitly tie it to the environmental pressure.

questions.prompt: The single ancestor from which two or more species both descended — the node where their branches join on a cladogram.
questions.explanation: A common ancestor is a species (often extinct, sometimes still living) from which two or more current species descended. On a cladogram or phylogenetic tree, the common ancestor is the branch point (node) where two lineages diverge. The most recent common ancestor of two species lies at their lowest shared node. Universal common ancestry — the claim that all life on Earth shares a single most-distant common ancestor (LUCA, the Last Universal Common Ancestor) — is supported by the universality of DNA, the genetic code, ATP, ribosomes, and core metabolic pathways across all known life. NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely asks students to identify the most recent common ancestor of two species on a cladogram.

questions.prompt: An organism that makes its own food from sunlight or chemicals — the base of every food chain; green plants and algae are the main ones.
questions.explanation: Producers (autotrophs) build their own food from inorganic raw materials and an energy source. Most are photoautotrophs that use sunlight: green plants, algae, and cyanobacteria, all carrying out photosynthesis (6 CO2 + 6 H2O + light energy yields C6H12O6 + 6 O2). A smaller group are chemoautotrophs — bacteria around deep-sea hydrothermal vents that use chemical energy from H2S or methane instead of sunlight. Producers are the foundation of nearly every food chain on Earth: only the energy they capture flows on to the consumers above them. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses producers in every energy pyramid and food web question.

questions.prompt: An organism that obtains energy by eating other organisms — herbivores, carnivores, and omnivores all fall in this category.
questions.explanation: Consumers (heterotrophs) cannot make their own food and must consume other organisms. Primary consumers (first level above producers) are herbivores eating plants — deer, rabbits, grasshoppers, zooplankton. Secondary consumers eat primary consumers — wolves eating rabbits, frogs eating grasshoppers. Tertiary consumers eat secondary consumers — hawks eating snakes. Omnivores (humans, bears) eat both plants and animals; carnivores eat only meat; detritivores eat dead organic material. Only about 10% of energy at one level passes to the next, so food chains rarely exceed 4-5 levels. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses consumers in food webs and energy-pyramid questions.

questions.prompt: An organism — usually a fungus or bacterium — that breaks down dead plant and animal matter, releasing nutrients back to the soil.
questions.explanation: Decomposers break down dead organisms and organic waste, releasing nutrients back into the ecosystem in forms that producers can reuse. The two main groups are fungi (mushrooms, molds) and bacteria. Without decomposers, dead matter would pile up indefinitely and the limited supply of essential elements (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus) would lock up in dead bodies, halting all new life. Decomposers are critical to the carbon, nitrogen, and water cycles. Detritivores (earthworms, scavengers, dung beetles) physically break apart material to make it accessible to decomposers. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses decomposers in food-web diagrams and biogeochemical cycle questions.

questions.prompt: An animal that eats only plants — like a cow, a deer, or a grasshopper.
questions.explanation: An herbivore is an animal whose diet is exclusively or primarily plants. Herbivores include large mammals (deer, elk, cattle, horses, elephants), small mammals (rabbits, mice eating seeds), insects (grasshoppers, caterpillars), and zooplankton in water (eating phytoplankton). Herbivores typically have specialized teeth (broad molars for grinding plant material), long digestive tracts to extract nutrients from tough plant cellulose, and many have specialized gut bacteria or chambered stomachs (cows have four-chambered stomachs) to break down cellulose. Herbivores are primary consumers — the first level above producers in a food chain. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses herbivores in food chain diagrams.

questions.prompt: An animal that hunts and eats other animals — like a lion, a hawk, or a shark.
questions.explanation: A predator is an animal that hunts, kills, and consumes other animals (prey). Predators include large carnivores (wolves, lions, tigers, sharks, eagles), smaller carnivores (snakes, frogs, spiders, owls), and omnivores when they're hunting (bears, humans). Predator-prey relationships drive natural selection on both sides: predators evolve speed, stealth, and sharp senses; prey evolve speed, camouflage, alertness, and group living. Population-cycle graphs show predator and prey numbers rising and falling out of phase — when prey are abundant, predators thrive and grow in number, which then drives prey numbers down, which drives predator numbers down, and the cycle repeats. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses predator-prey graphs constantly.

questions.prompt: A simple sequence showing who eats whom — usually written grass to grasshopper to frog to hawk.
questions.explanation: A food chain is a linear sequence showing the flow of energy and nutrients from one organism to the next: producer to primary consumer to secondary consumer to tertiary consumer, with decomposers eventually breaking down all of them. Arrows point in the direction of energy flow (from prey to predator). About 10% of the energy at each level is passed to the next level — the rest is lost as heat during metabolism, used for growth, or excreted. This 10% rule limits food chains to ~4-5 levels and explains why top predators are relatively rare. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses food chains in nearly every ecology question.

questions.prompt: A network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem — more realistic than a simple chain because most animals eat multiple things.
questions.explanation: A food web is a network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Most animals eat multiple species and are eaten by multiple species, making real food relationships look more like a web than a chain. Food webs include producers, primary consumers, secondary consumers, tertiary consumers, and decomposers. Removing one species can ripple through the entire web — a keystone species (sea otters in kelp forests, wolves in Yellowstone) has effects out of proportion to its abundance. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 routinely shows a food web and asks students to predict how removing one species will affect others, or to identify all the primary consumers, secondary consumers, or producers.

questions.prompt: A diagram showing how much energy is available at each trophic level — wide at the producer base, narrow at the top.
questions.explanation: An energy pyramid (also called a trophic pyramid) shows how energy is distributed across the trophic levels of an ecosystem. The producer level (base) is the widest because producers capture the most energy from sunlight. Each successive level (primary consumer, secondary consumer, tertiary consumer) is about 10% the size of the level below — because most energy is lost as heat from metabolism, used for movement, or excreted. This is why top predators are rare and why short food chains support more biomass than long ones. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses energy pyramids and asks students to identify producer/consumer levels and predict effects of removing one level.

questions.prompt: The total mass of living matter in a given area or at a given trophic level — measured in grams or kilograms per square meter.
questions.explanation: Biomass is the total mass of living matter (or recently dead organic matter) in a given area or ecosystem. Biomass pyramids — like energy pyramids — usually show producers having the most biomass, primary consumers next, and so on up. Biomass is one way to measure how much living material an ecosystem can support; tropical rainforests have higher biomass than tundras. Biomass also refers to plant material used as fuel (firewood, ethanol from corn) — an example of converting solar energy first captured by photosynthesis into usable energy for humans. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses biomass pyramids in energy-flow questions.

questions.prompt: The position an organism occupies in a food chain — producer, primary consumer, secondary consumer, and so on.
questions.explanation: A trophic level is the position an organism occupies in a food chain or food web based on its energy source. Level 1: producers (photosynthetic plants, algae, cyanobacteria). Level 2: primary consumers (herbivores eating producers). Level 3: secondary consumers (carnivores eating primary consumers). Level 4: tertiary consumers (top predators). Decomposers operate at every level breaking down dead material from each. Energy and biomass decrease about 10x with each step up because so much energy is lost as heat between levels. NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to identify the trophic level of named organisms and explain why energy decreases with each level.

questions.prompt: The maximum number of individuals of a species an environment can support over the long term — set by food, water, space, and disease.
questions.explanation: Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely, given available food, water, shelter, and other resources. When a population is below K it grows; near K it stabilizes; above K it crashes. The classic graph of carrying capacity is the S-shaped logistic curve: exponential growth at first, then a leveling off near K. Carrying capacity is not fixed — drought, disease, habitat destruction, or invasive species can all lower it. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 routinely shows a population-growth graph and asks students to identify the carrying capacity (the upper plateau) and explain why the population stops growing there.

questions.prompt: Any factor that restricts population growth — food shortage, drought, disease, predators, or lack of space.
questions.explanation: A limiting factor is any environmental condition that restricts the growth, abundance, or distribution of a population. Density-dependent limiting factors (competition, predation, disease, parasitism) have stronger effects as the population grows denser. Density-independent factors (weather, natural disasters, climate) affect populations regardless of density. Liebig's law of the minimum says that population growth is limited by whichever resource is scarcest — even if everything else is abundant. NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to identify limiting factors in a scenario and predict how changes in those factors will shift carrying capacity.

questions.prompt: The number of individuals of a species per unit area — measured in fish per hectare, deer per square kilometer, or trees per acre.
questions.explanation: Population density is the number of individuals of a species per unit area or volume. Density matters because high density tends to intensify density-dependent limiting factors — disease spreads faster, competition for food and mates increases, and waste accumulates. Methods for measuring population density include direct counts in small areas, sampling with quadrats, and capture-mark-recapture studies. NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to compute population density from data, and to predict how density affects competition, disease spread, and population stability.

questions.prompt: The struggle between two or more individuals or species for the same limited resource — food, water, mates, or space.
questions.explanation: Competition occurs when two or more organisms need the same limited resource. Intraspecific competition (within a species) is generally more intense because individuals need exactly the same resource. Interspecific competition (between different species) can drive niche separation — two competing species often evolve to use slightly different resources to coexist (resource partitioning). The competitive exclusion principle states that two species cannot indefinitely occupy exactly the same niche; one will outcompete the other or evolve a different niche. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses competition in evolutionary, ecological, and biodiversity questions.

questions.prompt: A close, long-term relationship between two species — can be mutualism, commensalism, or parasitism.
questions.explanation: Symbiosis is any close, long-term biological interaction between two species. The three main types: (1) Mutualism — both species benefit (lichens are fungi + algae; gut bacteria help us digest food while feeding on what we eat; pollinators get nectar while moving pollen). (2) Commensalism — one species benefits, the other is unaffected (barnacles on whales). (3) Parasitism — one species benefits at the other's expense (tapeworms in intestines, fleas on dogs, mistletoe on trees). Lynn Margulis's endosymbiotic theory (1967) extended symbiosis to evolutionary timescales: mitochondria and chloroplasts descend from once-free-living bacteria that became permanent symbionts inside ancestral eukaryotes. NY Living Environment Regents tests symbiosis on Part A and Part B.

questions.prompt: The continuous movement of water through Earth's systems — evaporation from oceans, condensation into clouds, precipitation as rain or snow, and runoff back to the sea.
questions.explanation: The water cycle (hydrologic cycle) moves water continuously between the atmosphere, oceans, surface water, ground water, and living organisms. Key processes: evaporation (liquid water becomes vapor — mostly from oceans); transpiration (plants release water vapor through stomata); condensation (vapor becomes droplets, forming clouds); precipitation (rain, snow, sleet, hail); infiltration (water sinks into soil); runoff (surface flow back to rivers and oceans); deep groundwater storage. The cycle is powered by solar energy and gravity. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 uses water-cycle diagrams; students must identify and label the key processes and explain how human activities like deforestation alter the cycle.

questions.prompt: The movement of carbon through producers, consumers, decomposers, the atmosphere, oceans, and fossil fuels.
questions.explanation: The carbon cycle moves carbon among the atmosphere (mostly as CO2), oceans (as dissolved CO2 and carbonates), living organisms (in organic molecules), soils, and fossil fuels. Key processes: photosynthesis takes CO2 out of the atmosphere; cellular respiration returns it; decomposition releases carbon from dead matter; combustion of fossil fuels releases stored carbon back into the atmosphere; oceans absorb CO2 (becoming more acidic in the process). Burning fossil fuels at industrial scale has pushed atmospheric CO2 from ~280 ppm before 1750 to over 420 ppm today, driving the greenhouse effect and climate change. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 uses carbon-cycle diagrams in climate change questions.

questions.prompt: The cycle of nitrogen — atmospheric N2 fixed by bacteria into usable forms, then consumed by plants, eaten by animals, and returned to the soil and atmosphere.
questions.explanation: The nitrogen cycle moves nitrogen among the atmosphere (78% N2 gas — unusable by most organisms), soil, water, and living tissue. Key steps: nitrogen fixation (bacteria in soil and in legume root nodules convert N2 to ammonia, NH3); nitrification (other bacteria convert ammonia to nitrites, then nitrates that plants can absorb); assimilation (plants and animals build nitrogen into proteins and DNA); ammonification (decomposers convert dead-organism nitrogen back to ammonia); denitrification (bacteria return N2 to the atmosphere). Industrial nitrogen fixation through the Haber-Bosch process (used in synthetic fertilizers) has doubled the global nitrogen flux. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 uses nitrogen-cycle diagrams.

questions.prompt: The breakdown of dead organic matter by fungi, bacteria, and detritivores, releasing nutrients back into the soil.
questions.explanation: Decomposition is the breakdown of dead plant and animal matter into smaller molecules and ultimately back into inorganic compounds (CO2, water, ammonia, phosphates). Decomposers — primarily bacteria and fungi — secrete digestive enzymes onto the dead material, then absorb the products. Detritivores (earthworms, dung beetles, scavengers) physically break apart material first, increasing surface area. Decomposition releases stored nutrients back into the soil for producers to reuse, closing the loops of the carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus cycles. Without decomposition, nutrients would lock up in dead tissue and producers would starve. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses decomposition in food-web and biogeochemical-cycle questions.

questions.prompt: The continuous reuse of essential elements — carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water — through ecosystems by producers, consumers, and decomposers.
questions.explanation: Nutrient recycling is the continuous movement of essential elements through living systems and back to the physical environment. Unlike energy (which flows one-way through ecosystems and is lost as heat), nutrients cycle: producers absorb them from soil/water/air, consumers ingest them by eating producers and each other, decomposers release them from dead matter back into the soil and air for producers to reuse. The carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water cycles are all examples. Disrupting one cycle (e.g., burning fossil fuels accelerates the carbon cycle releasing stored carbon faster than it can be reabsorbed) destabilizes ecosystems. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses nutrient recycling in cycle-comparison questions.

questions.prompt: The predictable change in a community after a disturbance — bare rock becomes lichens, then mosses, then grasses, then forest.
questions.explanation: Ecological succession is the gradual, predictable change in a community over time after a disturbance. Primary succession starts on bare rock with no soil — after a glacier retreats, a volcanic eruption, or a sandbar emerges — beginning with pioneer species like lichens and mosses that break rock into soil; then grasses, shrubs, fast-growing trees, and eventually a mature climax community. Secondary succession starts where soil already exists but vegetation was wiped out — after a fire, flood, or abandoned farm — and proceeds faster because soil and seed banks remain. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses succession to test community-level dynamics and the role of pioneer species.

questions.prompt: A numerical measure of the variety of species in an ecosystem — counts both species number (richness) and how evenly they're distributed.
questions.explanation: A biodiversity index quantifies the variety of species in an ecosystem. The simplest measure is species richness (just the count of species present). More sophisticated indices like the Shannon Diversity Index also account for evenness — whether individuals are evenly spread across species or concentrated in just a few. High biodiversity makes ecosystems more resilient: more species means more functional redundancy, so losing one species is less devastating. The NYSED required Relationships and Biodiversity lab uses simulated species data to teach students how to assess relatedness; many Regents Part B questions ask students to compare biodiversity in two ecosystems based on counts and species lists.

questions.prompt: A non-native species that spreads aggressively in a new ecosystem, often outcompeting native species — zebra mussels and kudzu are classic examples.
questions.explanation: An invasive species is a non-native organism that, when introduced to a new ecosystem, spreads rapidly because it lacks natural predators, parasites, or competitors and outcompetes native species — often causing ecological and economic damage. Classic examples: zebra mussels in the Great Lakes (clog water-intake pipes), Burmese pythons in the Everglades (decimating native mammals), kudzu vine in the southeastern U.S. (smothering forests), emerald ash borer beetle (killing millions of ash trees), and Asian carp in U.S. rivers. Invasive species are a major threat to biodiversity. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 routinely asks students to predict the effect of an introduced species on a native food web.

questions.prompt: The breaking up of a continuous habitat into smaller, isolated patches — by roads, farms, or housing developments.
questions.explanation: Habitat fragmentation occurs when a large continuous habitat is broken into smaller, isolated patches — typically by roads, agriculture, urbanization, or logging. Fragments support smaller populations more vulnerable to extinction. They have more edge habitat (less interior), which disadvantages species that need deep interior conditions. Animals struggle to migrate between fragments to find mates or new territory, reducing gene flow and increasing inbreeding. Wildlife corridors (strips of natural habitat connecting fragments) are one conservation tool. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses fragmentation in human-impact questions, often paired with deforestation and other habitat-loss drivers.

questions.prompt: An ecosystem's ability to bounce back after disturbance — depends on biodiversity, intact food webs, and presence of native species.
questions.explanation: Ecosystem resilience is the ability of an ecosystem to absorb a disturbance — fire, drought, storm, disease outbreak, invasive species — and return to its previous state and function. High biodiversity boosts resilience: with many species filling overlapping roles, losing one species doesn't collapse the whole system. Diverse ecosystems also have more chance to contain species that can survive a particular stress. Low-biodiversity ecosystems (monoculture cropland, simplified urban ecosystems) are far less resilient. Climate change is testing resilience worldwide, with reefs bleaching, forests burning, and tundras melting. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses resilience in biodiversity and conservation questions.

questions.prompt: Pollution that comes from a single, identifiable source — like a factory's smokestack or a single drainage pipe discharging into a river.
questions.explanation: Point-source pollution comes from a single identifiable origin — a factory smokestack, a wastewater discharge pipe, an oil spill from a single tanker, a chemical plant leak. Because the source is identifiable and located in one place, point-source pollution is easier to regulate, monitor, and clean up. The U.S. Clean Water Act (1972) and Clean Air Act (1970) impose permits, emission limits, and reporting requirements on point sources. The 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil spill is a famous example of point-source water pollution. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses pollution-source identification in human-impact questions and asks students to distinguish point vs. nonpoint sources.

questions.prompt: Pollution that comes from many scattered sources — like fertilizer runoff from thousands of farms, oil drips from millions of cars, or pet waste in cities.
questions.explanation: Nonpoint-source pollution comes from many diffuse sources scattered over a wide area — agricultural runoff carrying fertilizer and pesticide from millions of acres of farmland; oil and gasoline drips from millions of cars rinsing off roads in stormwater; pet waste, fertilizer, and lawn chemicals running off suburban yards; sediment from construction sites; atmospheric deposition of pollutants over wide regions. Nonpoint sources are harder to regulate because no single polluter is to blame. The Chesapeake Bay's chronic nitrogen pollution and Gulf of Mexico 'dead zone' (from Mississippi River agricultural runoff) are classic examples. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests this distinction.

questions.prompt: Pollution caused when sulfur and nitrogen oxides from burning fossil fuels combine with water vapor to fall as a low-pH solution.
questions.explanation: Acid rain forms when sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NOx) emitted by coal-burning power plants, vehicles, and industry react with water vapor in the atmosphere to form sulfuric and nitric acids. These acids fall back to Earth as rain, snow, or fog with pH below 5.6 (normal rain pH ~5.6 from natural CO2). Acid rain damages forests (especially conifers and at high elevations), acidifies lakes (killing fish), erodes stone buildings and statues, and dissolves toxic metals like aluminum from soil. The U.S. Acid Rain Program (1990 Clean Air Act amendments) cut SO2 emissions sharply. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses acid rain in human-impact questions.

questions.prompt: Tiny solid particles or liquid droplets suspended in the air — soot, dust, smoke — small enough to be inhaled into the lungs.
questions.explanation: Particulate matter (PM) is tiny solid or liquid particles suspended in the air. PM10 (particles smaller than 10 micrometers) are inhalable; PM2.5 (smaller than 2.5 micrometers) penetrate deep into the lungs and even cross into the bloodstream. Sources include burning fossil fuels and wood, vehicle exhaust, construction dust, wildfires, and industrial processes. Health effects include asthma, heart disease, stroke, and premature death — particularly in urban environments with heavy traffic. The EPA monitors PM2.5 as one of the six criteria air pollutants. NY Living Environment Regents Part B includes particulate matter in air-pollution and human-health questions.

questions.prompt: Pollution from heated water released into a river or lake — usually from power plants or factories using water for cooling.
questions.explanation: Thermal pollution is the release of warm water (or sometimes very cold water) into a natural body of water, raising or lowering its temperature beyond what aquatic life can tolerate. Power plants — coal, gas, nuclear — use enormous amounts of river or lake water for cooling, then discharge it back several degrees warmer. Warmer water holds less dissolved oxygen (think: warm soda goes flat faster), stressing fish and other aerobic aquatic life. It can also alter spawning cycles and favor invasive species that tolerate warmer water. Solutions include cooling towers and using treated wastewater for cooling. NY Living Environment Regents Part B includes thermal pollution in human-impact questions.

questions.prompt: A natural resource that can be replenished by natural processes on a human timescale — solar, wind, hydropower, biomass.
questions.explanation: A renewable resource is one that naturally replenishes within a human timescale (years, not millions of years). Examples include solar energy, wind, flowing water, geothermal heat, biomass (firewood, ethanol, biogas), and sustainably-harvested timber and fish. Renewable resources can still be depleted if used faster than they regenerate (overfishing, clear-cutting). The challenge in transitioning to renewables is scale, intermittency (sun doesn't always shine, wind doesn't always blow), storage, and the materials needed for batteries and panels. NY Living Environment Regents Part B distinguishes renewable from nonrenewable resources in human-impact and energy questions, and tests their environmental tradeoffs.

questions.prompt: A natural resource that takes millions of years to form and cannot be replenished on a human timescale — coal, oil, natural gas, uranium.
questions.explanation: Nonrenewable resources cannot be replenished within a human timescale. Fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) formed from buried organic matter under heat and pressure over hundreds of millions of years. Uranium for nuclear power is finite. Mineral deposits (iron, copper, lithium) replenish on geological timescales. Burning fossil fuels powers modern civilization but releases CO2 and other pollutants. Once burned, that ancient stored solar energy is gone. The world has consumed fossil fuels at an accelerating rate since the 1750 Industrial Revolution. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses nonrenewable resources in human-impact, climate-change, and sustainable-development questions.

questions.prompt: Harvesting a renewable resource at a rate that allows it to be naturally replenished — so the supply doesn't decrease over time.
questions.explanation: Sustainable yield is harvesting a renewable resource at or below its natural replacement rate so the resource pool stays stable. In fisheries, the maximum sustainable yield is the largest annual catch that doesn't reduce the breeding population. In forestry, sustainable logging removes trees no faster than the forest can regrow them. Failure to maintain sustainable yields leads to collapse — Atlantic cod off Newfoundland was overfished and collapsed in 1992, putting 35,000 people out of work. Sustainable agriculture, sustainable fisheries, and sustainable forestry are all attempts to balance human consumption with ecosystem regeneration. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses sustainability in resource-use questions.

questions.prompt: The widespread cutting down of forests — for lumber, agriculture, mining, or development — currently devastating tropical rainforests.
questions.explanation: Deforestation is the large-scale removal of forest cover. Major drivers include conversion to agriculture (palm-oil plantations in Southeast Asia, cattle ranching and soy production in the Amazon), commercial logging, mining, and urban expansion. Effects include loss of biodiversity (tropical forests hold over half of land-species diversity), increased atmospheric CO2 (forests are major carbon stores; burning them releases that carbon), soil erosion, disrupted water cycles, and displacement of indigenous communities. The Amazon, Congo Basin, and Indonesian rainforests are losing area at alarming rates. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses deforestation in climate change, biodiversity, and human-impact questions.

questions.prompt: Catching fish faster than they can reproduce — collapses fish populations and disrupts entire marine food webs.
questions.explanation: Overfishing is the harvesting of fish at a rate that prevents the population from replenishing itself. Industrial fishing fleets with sonar, large nets, and freezer capacity have decimated many fish stocks. The Atlantic cod fishery off Newfoundland collapsed in 1992 after centuries of intensive fishing; over 35,000 workers lost their jobs and the population has still not fully recovered three decades later. Bluefin tuna, swordfish, and many shark species are heavily depleted. Solutions include fishing quotas, marine protected areas where fishing is banned to let populations recover, gear restrictions, and consumer pressure for sustainably-sourced seafood. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses overfishing in resource-use and biodiversity questions.

questions.prompt: A gas in the atmosphere that traps heat — carbon dioxide, methane, water vapor, and nitrous oxide are the main ones.
questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases (GHGs) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation (heat), keeping Earth warm enough to be habitable — natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth's average temperature near 15 degrees Celsius rather than below freezing. The main GHGs are water vapor, carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), and synthetic gases like CFCs. Human activities — burning fossil fuels, deforestation, livestock production, agriculture — have raised atmospheric CO2 from 280 ppm in 1750 to over 420 ppm today, driving anthropogenic climate change. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses greenhouse gases in climate change questions, often paired with the carbon cycle and human energy use.

questions.prompt: A measure of the greenhouse gases released by a person, organization, or activity — driving lifestyle choices around climate.
questions.explanation: A carbon footprint is the total amount of greenhouse gases (measured in CO2-equivalents) emitted by an individual, organization, product, or activity. Components include direct emissions (driving, heating, cooking with gas) and indirect emissions (electricity from coal-fired plants, food production, clothing manufacture, shipping). The average U.S. carbon footprint is about 16 tons CO2-equivalent per person per year, several times the global average. Reducing footprints involves transportation choices (driving less, electric vehicles), dietary choices (less beef), energy efficiency (LED lights, better insulation), and renewable-energy adoption. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses carbon footprint in human-impact and personal-decision questions.

questions.prompt: The long-term rise in Earth's average temperature caused mostly by human greenhouse-gas emissions — already measured at about 1.2 degrees C since 1880.
questions.explanation: Global warming is the long-term increase in Earth's average surface temperature due primarily to human emissions of greenhouse gases (especially CO2, CH4, N2O) from burning fossil fuels, deforestation, agriculture, and industry. Since 1880, Earth has warmed about 1.2 degrees C; without major emission cuts, additional warming of several degrees is projected by 2100. Effects include sea-level rise from melting ice and thermal expansion, more frequent and intense heat waves, droughts, floods, wildfires, ocean acidification, range shifts in species, and increased extinction. Climate scientists overwhelmingly attribute current warming to human activity. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses global warming in climate-change questions.

questions.prompt: The gradual increase in ocean height — caused by melting glaciers, melting polar ice, and the thermal expansion of warmer water.
questions.explanation: Sea-level rise is the increase in average ocean height caused by climate change. Two main mechanisms: (1) thermal expansion — water expands as it warms, taking up more volume; (2) ice melt — glaciers, ice sheets in Greenland and Antarctica, and Arctic sea ice melting and adding water to the oceans. Sea level has risen ~20 cm (8 inches) since 1900, and projections range from another 0.3 to over 1 meter by 2100 depending on emission rates. Effects include flooding of low-lying coastal cities (Miami, New Orleans, New York), island nations (Maldives, Tuvalu), and the salinization of coastal aquifers. NY Living Environment Regents Part B includes sea-level rise in climate questions.

questions.prompt: Actions taken to reduce or limit greenhouse gas emissions — renewable energy, energy efficiency, reforestation, electrified transportation.
questions.explanation: Climate mitigation means reducing greenhouse-gas emissions to slow or stop further warming. Key strategies: replacing fossil fuels with renewables (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, nuclear); improving energy efficiency in buildings, transportation, and industry; electrifying transportation (electric vehicles); reforestation and afforestation to absorb CO2; capturing carbon at industrial sources; reducing methane from agriculture and waste; and behavioral changes like reduced beef consumption. Mitigation contrasts with adaptation, which means coping with climate change that already cannot be prevented (sea walls, drought-tolerant crops, cooling centers). The Paris Agreement (2015) is the main international mitigation framework. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses mitigation in policy questions.

questions.prompt: The destruction of the natural environment a species depends on — the #1 cause of extinction worldwide.
questions.explanation: Habitat loss is the destruction or significant alteration of the natural environment a species needs to survive. Drivers include conversion of forests, wetlands, and grasslands to cropland and pasture; urban and suburban expansion; mining; road construction; and dam-building. Habitat loss is the leading driver of biodiversity loss globally — the IUCN estimates it threatens 85% of species at risk of extinction. Tropical deforestation alone is wiping out countless species, many never even described. Once habitat is gone, species cannot survive there no matter how much we protect them elsewhere. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses habitat loss in conservation, biodiversity, and human-impact questions.

questions.prompt: A species brought (deliberately or accidentally) by humans to a new region where it does not naturally occur — often becomes invasive.
questions.explanation: An introduced species (non-native, exotic, alien species) is a species that humans have brought — deliberately or accidentally — to a new region outside its native range. Not all introduced species become invasive; many fail to establish or remain harmless. But some — without their natural predators, parasites, or competitors — explode in population and harm native species and ecosystems. Examples: zebra mussels arriving in Great Lakes via ballast water; the European starling deliberately released in Central Park (NYC) in 1890; kudzu vine introduced to control erosion. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses introduced species in biodiversity and food-web disruption questions.

questions.prompt: The build-up of a toxin (like mercury or DDT) inside one organism over its lifetime — gets into the body faster than it can be excreted.
questions.explanation: Bioaccumulation is the buildup of a persistent, fat-soluble toxin (mercury, DDT, PCBs) inside an organism's body over its lifetime, faster than it can be metabolized or excreted. The organism absorbs the toxin through food, water, or air, and stores it (often in fatty tissue). Concentrations rise as the organism ages. Bioaccumulation is the first step in biomagnification, where toxins concentrate up the food chain. Mercury bioaccumulates particularly in long-lived predator fish (tuna, swordfish, king mackerel), leading to FDA advisories on consumption. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses bioaccumulation in food-chain pollution questions.

questions.prompt: The way toxins become more concentrated at each higher level of a food chain — eagles and tuna can have toxin levels millions of times higher than the water around them.
questions.explanation: Biomagnification is the increase in toxin concentration at each successive trophic level of a food chain. Each consumer eats many prey, each carrying some toxin; the toxin accumulates in the consumer at a higher concentration than in any single prey. Top predators end up with the highest concentrations — often millions of times the surrounding water. DDT biomagnification (documented by Rachel Carson in Silent Spring, 1962) caused bald eagle, peregrine falcon, and brown pelican populations to crash from thin eggshells; DDT was banned in the U.S. in 1972 and these species recovered. Mercury still biomagnifies in fish, leading to human exposure. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses biomagnification.

questions.prompt: A species at high risk of going extinct in the near future — wild populations are small and shrinking.
questions.explanation: An endangered species is one at serious risk of extinction throughout all or a significant portion of its range. The U.S. Endangered Species Act (1973) provides legal protection — banning hunting and trade and requiring habitat protection — and has helped recover species like the bald eagle, California condor, and Florida manatee. The IUCN Red List categorizes species globally from Least Concern to Critically Endangered to Extinct. Currently endangered species include the giant panda, mountain gorilla, Sumatran tiger, Amur leopard, vaquita porpoise, and many more. Causes include habitat loss, poaching, climate change, and disease. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses endangered species in conservation questions.

questions.prompt: The protection, preservation, and careful management of natural resources and biodiversity — national parks, wildlife refuges, marine protected areas.
questions.explanation: Conservation is the protection and management of natural resources, ecosystems, and species — combining ecology with public policy. Tools include national parks and wildlife refuges (Yellowstone established 1872), endangered species protection (1973 U.S. Endangered Species Act), wildlife corridors, captive-breeding programs, habitat restoration, sustainable harvest quotas, and consumer awareness campaigns. Conservation success stories include the recovery of the bald eagle (after DDT was banned), American bison (from near extinction in 1900), and humpback whales (after commercial whaling moratorium). Conservation faces ongoing tradeoffs with economic development, jobs, and indigenous rights. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses conservation broadly in human-impact and biodiversity questions.

questions.prompt: The collection, processing, and reuse of materials — paper, aluminum, plastic, glass — that would otherwise become waste.
questions.explanation: Recycling is the collection, processing, and reuse of waste materials to make new products — paper, aluminum cans, steel, glass, certain plastics, electronics. Aluminum recycling saves about 95% of the energy needed to make new aluminum from bauxite ore. Recycled paper saves trees, water, and landfill space. Glass and steel can be recycled indefinitely without loss of quality. Plastic recycling is more limited — only some types (PET #1, HDPE #2) are widely recycled; many plastics are downcycled or end up as waste. Recycling reduces but does not eliminate environmental impact — the best strategy is reduce first, reuse second, recycle third. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses recycling in sustainability questions.

questions.prompt: A formal document required before major projects — describes expected environmental effects so decision-makers can weigh costs and benefits.
questions.explanation: An Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) is a detailed document, required under the U.S. National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA, 1970), that assesses the likely environmental effects of major proposed government actions or projects — pipelines, dams, highways, mines, large developments. The EIS describes the project, the existing environment, expected effects (on air, water, wildlife, human communities), alternative approaches, and ways to reduce harm (mitigation). Public comment and review are required. NY also has its own State Environmental Quality Review Act (SEQRA, 1975) covering state and local projects. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses EISs in environmental policy and human-impact questions.

questions.prompt: An analytical method comparing the expected costs (economic, social, environmental) and benefits of a decision — helps choose among policy options.
questions.explanation: Cost-benefit analysis (CBA) systematically compares the expected costs and benefits of a decision — economic, social, and environmental — to help choose among options. CBAs assign monetary values to outcomes where possible (jobs created, health-care savings, agricultural production) and provide qualitative descriptions for outcomes that are harder to monetize (species loss, scenic beauty, cultural heritage). Tradeoffs are central — almost every environmental decision benefits some people and harms others. CBAs are used in EISs, in setting pollution standards, in cap-and-trade markets, and in carbon pricing. Critics note that CBA can undervalue what isn't easy to monetize. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses tradeoffs in environmental-decision questions.

questions.prompt: Returning a damaged ecosystem to a functioning state — replanting native trees, removing dams, reintroducing native species.
questions.explanation: Ecological restoration is actively returning a damaged or degraded ecosystem toward its functioning, biodiverse state. Methods include replanting native plants, removing invasive species, reintroducing extirpated native species (gray wolves to Yellowstone in 1995, condors to California), reconnecting fragmented habitats with wildlife corridors, removing dams and restoring riverflows (Elwha River dam removal completed 2014), and re-creating wetlands. Restoration is often slow, expensive, and imperfect — but it can substantially recover ecological function over years to decades. The U.N. has declared 2021-2030 the Decade on Ecosystem Restoration. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses restoration as a solution-side counterpoint to extinction and habitat loss.

questions.prompt: A homeostatic mechanism that reverses a change to bring the body back to its set point — sweating when hot, shivering when cold.
questions.explanation: Negative feedback is the most common homeostatic mechanism. When a variable strays from its set point, sensors detect the change, the body sends signals to effectors, and the effectors act to reverse the change — bringing the variable back toward the set point. Examples: body temperature drops, you shiver (muscle contraction produces heat); blood sugar rises after a meal, the pancreas releases insulin (cells absorb glucose and blood sugar drops); blood pressure rises, blood vessels dilate (pressure falls). The system is 'negative' because it counteracts (reduces, negates) the disturbance. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 uses negative-feedback diagrams in homeostasis questions constantly.

questions.prompt: A homeostatic mechanism that amplifies a change rather than reversing it — like the cascade leading to childbirth or blood clotting.
questions.explanation: Positive feedback amplifies a change instead of reversing it — the response makes the change even bigger. Examples in healthy human physiology include childbirth (uterine contractions release oxytocin, which causes stronger contractions, releasing more oxytocin, until birth) and blood clotting (platelets at a wound site release chemicals that attract more platelets, until the clot is complete). Positive feedback is rare compared to negative feedback because it must terminate (when birth or clot formation is complete) — otherwise it spirals out of control. NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to distinguish negative from positive feedback in physiological scenarios.

questions.prompt: A specialized cell or organ that detects a stimulus — taste buds, retina cells, sensory neurons in the skin.
questions.explanation: A receptor is a cell or molecular structure that detects a specific stimulus and converts it into a signal the body can interpret. Sensory receptors include photoreceptors in the retina (light), mechanoreceptors in the skin (touch, pressure), chemoreceptors in taste buds and the nose (chemicals), thermoreceptors in skin and the brain (temperature), and pain receptors (tissue damage). At the molecular level, hormones bind to specific protein receptors on cell membranes, triggering specific responses. Without receptors, organisms could not detect or respond to internal or external changes. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses receptors in feedback-loop diagrams: the receptor detects the change, sending signal to effector.

questions.prompt: A muscle or gland that responds to a feedback signal — sweat glands that cool you, the pancreas that releases insulin to lower blood sugar.
questions.explanation: An effector is the muscle or gland that carries out the body's response in a feedback loop. After a receptor detects a change and a control center (often the hypothalamus or another part of the brain, or an endocrine gland) integrates the information and sends a signal, the effector takes action. Examples: sweat glands secreting sweat to cool the body; skeletal muscles contracting to shiver and produce heat; the pancreas releasing insulin to lower blood sugar; the adrenal medulla releasing adrenaline in 'fight or flight'. NY Living Environment Regents Part B feedback-loop diagrams label receptor, control center, and effector — students must identify each role.

questions.prompt: The target value of a homeostatic variable — body temperature near 37 degrees C, blood glucose near 90 mg/dL, blood pH near 7.4.
questions.explanation: A set point is the optimal target value of a homeostatic variable around which the body regulates. Examples: normal core body temperature is set at about 37 degrees Celsius (98.6 F); fasting blood glucose set point is about 90 mg/dL; blood pH set point is 7.4 (slightly basic). When a variable strays from its set point, negative feedback acts to return it. The set point can shift in some conditions — during fever, the hypothalamus resets the temperature set point higher (which is why you feel cold and shiver to reach the new, higher target), helping fight infection. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses set-point reasoning in homeostasis questions.

questions.prompt: The body system — heart, blood vessels, and blood — that transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and wastes throughout the body.
questions.explanation: The circulatory (cardiovascular) system has three main components: the heart (a four-chambered muscular pump), blood vessels (arteries, arterioles, capillaries, venules, veins), and blood. It moves oxygen from the lungs to every cell, nutrients from the digestive tract to every cell, hormones from glands to target cells, immune cells throughout the body, heat to maintain body temperature, and wastes (CO2, urea) to the lungs and kidneys for excretion. Humans have a closed double-loop circulation — one loop to the lungs (pulmonary) and one to the rest of the body (systemic). NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses circulation in homeostasis and gas-exchange questions.

questions.prompt: The straw-colored liquid portion of the blood — water with dissolved proteins, glucose, electrolytes, and waste products.
questions.explanation: Blood plasma is the straw-colored fluid that makes up about 55% of blood volume. It is roughly 92% water, plus dissolved proteins (albumin, antibodies, clotting factors), glucose, electrolytes (sodium, potassium, calcium), hormones, wastes, and gases. Red and white blood cells and platelets are suspended in plasma. Plasma serves as the transport medium for nutrients absorbed in the small intestine, hormones traveling from glands, antibodies fighting infection, and wastes heading to the kidneys. Plasma without clotting factors is called serum. NY Living Environment Regents Part A and B test plasma's composition and transport role.

questions.prompt: The iron-containing protein in red blood cells that binds and transports oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body.
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that binds oxygen in the lungs (where O2 concentration is high) and releases it to tissues (where O2 concentration is low). Each hemoglobin molecule has four iron-containing heme groups; one hemoglobin molecule carries up to four O2 molecules. Hemoglobin gives blood its red color. Mutations in the hemoglobin gene cause sickle-cell anemia (HbS) — red blood cells deform into a sickle shape, blocking capillaries; this is a classic Regents Part B example of how a single DNA base change alters one amino acid, changing protein shape, changing function. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests hemoglobin in oxygen-transport and genetic-disease questions.

questions.prompt: The exchange of gases, nutrients, and wastes between blood and body tissues at the smallest blood vessels, which are only one cell wall thick.
questions.explanation: Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels — one endothelial cell thick — where exchange of materials between blood and tissue occurs by diffusion. Oxygen, glucose, and other nutrients diffuse from blood into cells; CO2 and metabolic wastes diffuse from cells into blood. The thin capillary wall and the immense total surface area of all capillaries (~7000 m2 in a human, larger than a basketball court) make this exchange efficient. Capillary beds form between arterioles and venules in every tissue. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses capillary-exchange diagrams in respiratory, circulatory, and homeostasis questions, often paired with alveoli in lungs.

questions.prompt: The body system of vessels and lymph nodes that returns excess tissue fluid to the bloodstream and helps fight infection.
questions.explanation: The lymphatic system is a network of vessels and lymph nodes that returns excess interstitial fluid to the bloodstream (preventing tissue swelling, called edema) and plays a major role in immunity. Lymph nodes filter lymph and house lymphocytes (B cells, T cells) that mount immune responses against pathogens. The thymus, spleen, tonsils, and adenoids are part of the lymphatic system. The lymphatic system also absorbs dietary fats from the small intestine via lacteals. When you have a swollen 'gland' in your neck during a cold, that's actually an enlarged lymph node responding to infection. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses the lymphatic system in immunity and homeostasis questions.

questions.prompt: The body's coordinated reaction to invading pathogens — produces antibodies, activates immune cells, and remembers the invader for next time.
questions.explanation: The immune response is the body's coordinated defense against invading pathogens. It has two main arms: innate immunity (fast, non-specific — skin, mucus, stomach acid, phagocytic white blood cells, fever) and adaptive immunity (slower, specific — B cells producing antibodies; T cells killing infected cells; memory cells for faster response on re-exposure). The adaptive system 'remembers' specific pathogens after first exposure, which is why you usually don't catch chicken pox twice and why vaccines work. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses immune responses in disease and vaccination questions, often paired with antibiotics and antibody-antigen specificity.

questions.prompt: A protein produced by white blood cells that binds specifically to a pathogen's molecules and marks it for destruction.
questions.explanation: An antibody (immunoglobulin) is a Y-shaped protein produced by B cells (a type of white blood cell) that recognizes and binds specifically to one molecular shape — an antigen — on a pathogen. The binding marks the pathogen for destruction by other immune cells, neutralizes the pathogen's ability to enter cells, or clumps pathogens together for easier elimination. Each B cell makes one antibody specific to one antigen; the immune system can generate billions of different antibodies. Memory B cells produced during first exposure provide fast antibody production on later re-exposure. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses antibodies in immunity, blood-typing, and vaccination questions.

questions.prompt: Any disease-causing organism — bacteria, virus, fungus, protozoan, parasitic worm.
questions.explanation: A pathogen is any organism or virus that can cause disease — bacteria (Streptococcus, E. coli), viruses (influenza, HIV, SARS-CoV-2), fungi (athlete's foot, ringworm, Candida), protozoa (malaria-causing Plasmodium, Giardia), and parasitic worms (tapeworms, roundworms, schistosomes). Pathogens enter the body through respiratory droplets, contaminated food and water, vectors like mosquitoes and ticks, sexual contact, or direct skin contact. Pathogen-host interactions drive much of the immune system's evolution. Louis Pasteur's germ theory (1860s) was the foundation of modern medicine, showing that microscopic pathogens — not 'bad air' — cause infectious diseases. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses pathogens in disease and immunity questions.

questions.prompt: A medical preparation containing a weakened, killed, or modified pathogen that trains the immune system to recognize and fight that pathogen.
questions.explanation: A vaccine is a preparation that exposes the immune system to a harmless form of a pathogen — a weakened or killed virus, a subunit protein, or mRNA encoding a viral protein — so that the body produces antibodies and memory cells without the person ever getting sick. On later exposure to the real pathogen, the immune system responds faster and stronger, preventing disease.
