Edward Jenner pioneered vaccination in 1796 using cowpox to prevent smallpox; vaccines have since eradicated smallpox (1980) and nearly eliminated polio, measles, and many other diseases. mRNA vaccines (COVID-19, 2020-21) are the newest technology. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses vaccines in immunity and biotechnology questions.

questions.prompt: When bacteria evolve to survive a drug that used to kill them — caused by overuse and misuse of antibiotics; a major public health crisis.
questions.explanation: Antibiotic resistance is the ability of bacteria to survive antibiotic treatment that would normally kill them. It evolves by natural selection: random mutations occasionally produce resistant bacterial cells; when antibiotics are used, susceptible bacteria die but resistant bacteria survive and reproduce, passing on the resistance allele. Overuse and misuse of antibiotics (incomplete courses, prescribing for viral infections, livestock feed antibiotics) accelerate resistance. MRSA, drug-resistant tuberculosis, and resistant gonorrhea are major public-health concerns. The CDC estimates 2.8 million U.S. antibiotic-resistant infections per year. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses antibiotic resistance as the clearest example of evolution by natural selection happening on a human timescale.

questions.prompt: The body system of brain, spinal cord, and nerves that uses electrical signals for fast communication and rapid responses.
questions.explanation: The nervous system consists of the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and peripheral nervous system (all other nerves). It senses internal and external conditions, processes information, and triggers responses — all using electrical signals (action potentials) traveling along neurons, with chemical signals (neurotransmitters) crossing synapses between cells. The nervous system enables fast responses (milliseconds), conscious thought, memory, learning, and emotion. It works with the endocrine system to maintain homeostasis. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses the nervous system in feedback-loop diagrams and contrasts its fast electrical signaling with the slower chemical signaling of the endocrine system.

questions.prompt: The body's system of glands that produce hormones — uses chemical signaling through the bloodstream for slower, longer-lasting effects.
questions.explanation: The endocrine system is the body's chemical communication network — a collection of glands (pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenals, pancreas, ovaries, testes) that secrete hormones into the bloodstream. Hormones travel to target cells with matching receptors and trigger responses. The endocrine system regulates growth (growth hormone), metabolism (thyroid hormones), blood sugar (insulin, glucagon), stress response (cortisol, adrenaline), and reproduction (estrogen, testosterone, FSH, LH). It works with the nervous system through the hypothalamus-pituitary axis. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses the endocrine system in homeostasis (especially blood sugar) and reproduction (menstrual cycle) questions.

questions.prompt: The specialized cell of the nervous system that conducts electrical signals between the brain, spinal cord, and the rest of the body.
questions.explanation: A neuron is the specialized cell that carries electrical signals through the nervous system. Three main parts: dendrites (receive signals from other neurons), the cell body (with nucleus, integrates signals), and the axon (carries the signal to the next neuron or to an effector). The signal — an action potential — travels along the axon as a wave of voltage change. Where neurons meet (synapse), the signal triggers release of neurotransmitters that cross to the next cell. Neurons can fire hundreds of times per second. Some axons are myelinated (wrapped in insulation) for faster conduction. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses neuron diagrams in nervous-system questions, asking for parts and function.

questions.prompt: A chemical messenger released by an endocrine gland into the bloodstream that affects target cells elsewhere.
questions.explanation: A hormone is a chemical messenger produced by an endocrine gland, released into the bloodstream, and transported to target cells — cells with specific receptors for that hormone. Examples: insulin (from pancreas, lowers blood glucose), glucagon (from pancreas, raises blood glucose), adrenaline/epinephrine (from adrenal medulla, fight-or-flight), thyroxine (from thyroid, controls metabolism), estrogen and testosterone (from gonads, sexual development and reproduction). Hormones act more slowly than nervous signals but have longer-lasting effects. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses hormones in homeostasis diagrams, especially blood-glucose regulation, and in reproductive and menstrual-cycle questions.

questions.prompt: The junction between two neurons where the electrical signal jumps from one to the next through chemical neurotransmitters.
questions.explanation: A synapse is the small gap (~20 nanometers) between two neurons where the electrical signal is converted to a chemical signal. When an action potential reaches the end of the axon, it triggers release of neurotransmitter molecules (dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, GABA, glutamate) into the synapse; the neurotransmitters cross and bind to receptors on the next cell's dendrite, triggering or inhibiting a new electrical signal. This chemical step is where many drugs and toxins act — caffeine blocks adenosine receptors, antidepressants alter serotonin or dopamine levels, nerve gas inhibits acetylcholine breakdown. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses synapse diagrams in nervous-system questions.

questions.prompt: A protein catalyst secreted by the digestive system that breaks down food molecules — amylase, lipase, pepsin, lactase are examples.
questions.explanation: Digestive enzymes are proteins that catalyze the breakdown of food into smaller absorbable molecules. Major examples: salivary amylase (starch to maltose, in the mouth); pepsin (proteins to peptides, in the stomach, optimal pH ~2); pancreatic amylase, lipase (fats to fatty acids), and trypsin (proteins to peptides) in the small intestine; lactase (lactose to glucose + galactose, on intestinal lining). Each enzyme has an optimum pH; stomach enzymes work best in acid, intestinal enzymes work best in slightly basic conditions. Enzyme function depends on protein shape — too high temperature denatures them. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests digestive enzymes with enzyme-activity graphs.

questions.prompt: The long, coiled organ where most chemical digestion and nearly all nutrient absorption take place; lined with villi for huge surface area.
questions.explanation: The small intestine is about 6 meters (20 feet) long in adults, organized into three sections: duodenum (where pancreatic enzymes and bile enter), jejunum, and ileum. Its inner lining is folded into circular folds, then villi (finger-like projections), then microvilli on each villus cell — giving a total absorptive surface area of about 250 m2 (the size of a tennis court). Nutrients (amino acids, monosaccharides, fatty acids) are absorbed across the villi into the blood (or, for fats, into lacteals of the lymphatic system). NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses small-intestine diagrams in digestion and surface-area questions.

questions.prompt: A tiny air sac in the lungs where oxygen diffuses into the blood and carbon dioxide diffuses out — about 300 million of them in each human lung.
questions.explanation: Alveoli (plural) are tiny grape-like air sacs at the ends of the smallest bronchioles in the lungs. Each is wrapped in capillaries and is one cell-layer thick — perfect for gas exchange by diffusion. Oxygen diffuses from alveolar air (where its concentration is higher) into capillary blood; CO2 diffuses from blood (where it's higher) into the alveoli to be exhaled. Each human lung holds roughly 300 million alveoli, giving a total gas-exchange surface area of about 70 m2 (the size of a tennis court) — an extreme example of high surface-area-to-volume ratio in service of efficient diffusion. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses alveoli diagrams in gas-exchange and respiratory-system questions.

questions.prompt: The dome-shaped muscle below the lungs that contracts to draw air in and relaxes to push air out — the main muscle of breathing.
questions.explanation: The diaphragm is a large dome-shaped sheet of skeletal muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdomen. When the diaphragm contracts, it flattens downward, expanding chest volume; this lowers pressure inside the lungs, and air rushes in (inhalation). When the diaphragm relaxes, the chest cavity shrinks, pressure rises, and air is pushed out (exhalation). The intercostal muscles between the ribs assist breathing. Hiccups are involuntary contractions of the diaphragm. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses the diaphragm in respiratory diagrams; students should understand inhalation and exhalation as a pressure-difference mechanism driven by diaphragm contraction.

questions.prompt: The microscopic filtering unit of the kidney — about a million per kidney — where blood is filtered and urine is formed.
questions.explanation: A nephron is the microscopic functional unit of the kidney; each human kidney has about 1 million nephrons. Each nephron has a glomerulus (a tight ball of capillaries) wrapped in a Bowman's capsule, where blood is filtered under pressure — water, ions, glucose, urea, and small molecules pass into the tubule, while blood cells and large proteins stay in the blood. The tubule then selectively reabsorbs glucose, salts, and water back into the blood while concentrating wastes (urea, excess salts) as urine. The kidney's careful balancing of filtration and reabsorption is central to homeostasis of water, blood pressure, pH, and electrolytes. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses nephron diagrams in excretion questions.

questions.prompt: The process by which initially identical cells take on specific roles — turning into nerve, muscle, blood, or other specialized cells.
questions.explanation: Cell specialization (differentiation) is the process by which initially unspecialized embryonic cells become specialized for specific functions — nerve cells, muscle cells, red blood cells, leaf cells, root cells. All body cells contain the same DNA, but each cell type expresses only the genes it needs, producing only the proteins needed for that job. Cell specialization is the foundation of multicellularity and the levels of organization (cells > tissues > organs > organ systems > organism). NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to explain how cells with the same DNA can have different shapes and functions — the answer is differential gene expression.

questions.prompt: The movement of substances across the cell membrane — by diffusion, osmosis, facilitated diffusion, or active transport.
questions.explanation: Membrane transport encompasses all the ways substances cross the cell membrane: passive transport — simple diffusion (small nonpolar molecules pass freely through the phospholipid bilayer), facilitated diffusion (larger or charged molecules pass through protein channels), and osmosis (water across a selectively permeable membrane); active transport — using ATP and protein pumps to move substances against their concentration gradient. The NYSED Diffusion Through a Membrane required lab demonstrates these directly with dialysis tubing and starch/iodine. NY Living Environment Regents Part B and Part D ask students to interpret diagrams and predict the direction and mechanism of transport.

questions.prompt: The way an enzyme works — binding to a specific substrate at its active site and speeding up a biochemical reaction.
questions.explanation: Enzyme action follows the lock-and-key (or induced-fit) model: a substrate molecule binds to the enzyme's specific active site, the enzyme catalyzes a reaction (lowering activation energy), and the product is released. Each enzyme has an optimum temperature and pH — outside that range, the protein denatures (loses its three-dimensional shape) and the active site no longer fits the substrate. Lactase breaks down lactose; amylase breaks down starch; pepsin breaks down proteins in the stomach. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses enzyme-activity graphs constantly — students must read the optimum, predict behavior off-optimum, and write Regents-style explanations involving the active site shape change.

questions.prompt: The way the human body systems work together — like the respiratory system delivering oxygen to the circulatory system, which transports it to every cell.
questions.explanation: Body systems interact constantly to maintain homeostasis and carry out the eight life functions. The respiratory system brings oxygen into the alveoli; the circulatory system picks it up from capillaries and delivers it to every cell while returning CO2 to the lungs. The digestive system absorbs nutrients in the small intestine, the circulatory system transports them, and the excretory system removes wastes via the kidney nephrons. The nervous and endocrine systems coordinate all the others through electrical and chemical signals. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 CRQ-style questions routinely ask students to describe how at least two body systems work together to respond to a stimulus (exercise, illness, dehydration).

questions.prompt: The negative-feedback adjustment that returns a body variable (temperature, glucose, pH) to its set point after a disturbance.
questions.explanation: A homeostatic response is the body's coordinated reaction to a change that pulls a variable away from its set point — the receptors detect the change, the brain or an endocrine gland integrates the signal, effectors (muscles or glands) act to reverse the change. Examples: blood sugar rises after a meal, pancreas releases insulin, cells absorb glucose, blood sugar drops; body temperature falls, hypothalamus signals shivering and vasoconstriction, body warms back up. Negative feedback is the most common mechanism. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 routinely shows a homeostatic-response diagram with stimulus, receptor, control center, effector, and response, asking students to label parts and explain the loop.

questions.prompt: The process by which the cell makes an exact copy of its DNA before cell division — uses base pairing rules and DNA polymerase.
questions.explanation: DNA replication is the process by which a cell duplicates its entire DNA before dividing. It is semi-conservative — each new DNA molecule contains one original strand and one newly synthesized strand. The double helix unwinds, helicase enzymes separate the strands, and DNA polymerase reads each template strand and adds complementary nucleotides (A pairs with T, G pairs with C). Errors are rare (~1 in a billion bases) thanks to proofreading; uncorrected errors are mutations. Replication occurs in S-phase of the cell cycle, before mitosis or meiosis. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses DNA-replication diagrams and the base-pairing rule to test students.

questions.prompt: The two-step process by which the DNA's coded message becomes a working protein — transcription in the nucleus, then translation at the ribosome.
questions.explanation: Protein synthesis converts the DNA code into a functioning protein in two steps. Transcription (in the nucleus): DNA is read by RNA polymerase, which produces a complementary messenger RNA (mRNA) strand using the bases A, U, G, C. Translation (at the ribosome, in the cytoplasm): the ribosome reads the mRNA three bases at a time (a codon), and each codon specifies one amino acid via the tRNA carrying it. Amino acids link in order to form a polypeptide, which folds into a functioning protein. A single base change (mutation) can change one codon, alter one amino acid, and dramatically change protein function (sickle-cell anemia is the classic example). NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses codon charts every year.

questions.prompt: The way a trait passes from parent to offspring — Mendelian, codominance, sex-linked, polygenic, and other modes.
questions.explanation: Inheritance patterns describe how traits move from parents to offspring. Simple Mendelian patterns: dominant-recessive with one gene having two alleles. Codominance: both alleles fully expressed (AB blood type). Incomplete dominance: heterozygote intermediate (pink flowers from red x white). Sex-linked: genes on X (mostly) — affects males more often (color blindness, hemophilia). Polygenic: many genes contribute (height, skin color, intelligence). Environmental influence: same genotype produces different phenotypes in different conditions. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses pedigree charts and Punnett squares to test these patterns — students must identify whether a trait is dominant or recessive, autosomal or sex-linked.

questions.prompt: How a change in DNA affects the protein and ultimately the organism — sometimes neutral, sometimes harmful, occasionally beneficial.
questions.explanation: A mutation's effect depends on what changes and where. Silent mutations change a codon to another codon for the same amino acid — no effect. Missense mutations change one amino acid; effects vary from minor to severe (sickle-cell anemia is missense). Nonsense mutations create a stop codon, truncating the protein. Frameshift mutations (insertions or deletions not in multiples of three) shift the reading frame, usually destroying protein function. Mutations in non-coding regions can affect gene expression. Most mutations in body cells affect only the individual; mutations in gametes are passed to offspring. Mutations are the ultimate source of new alleles on which natural selection acts. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests mutation effects in evolution and disease contexts.

questions.prompt: The scientific support — DNA fingerprinting, cloning, CRISPR — that biotechnology provides for medicine, agriculture, and forensics.
questions.explanation: Biotechnology provides evidence and applications across many fields. DNA fingerprinting (developed by Alec Jeffreys 1984) compares DNA profiles in forensic identification, paternity testing, and finding wrongfully convicted people. Cloning (Dolly the sheep, 1996) demonstrates that adult cells contain all the DNA needed for development. CRISPR-Cas9 (Doudna and Charpentier, Nobel Prize 2020) allows precise gene editing, with applications in treating sickle-cell disease, inherited blindness, and certain cancers. Genetic engineering produces human insulin in bacteria, Bt corn that resists pests, golden rice that produces vitamin A. Each biotechnology has benefits and ethical/safety tradeoffs. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests biotechnology applications.

questions.prompt: Any environmental condition that favors certain heritable traits over others — drives natural selection.
questions.explanation: A selection pressure (selective pressure, environmental pressure) is any environmental factor that affects an organism's chance of surviving and reproducing — predators, disease, food scarcity, drought, climate, competition, the presence of antibiotics for bacteria. Selection pressures act differently on different heritable variants in a population: those with traits that confer an advantage (a 'survival advantage') in the current environment survive at higher rates and leave more offspring. Over generations, advantageous traits become more common. The classic Beaks of Finches lab demonstrates how a selection pressure (different available food types) favors different beak shapes. NY Living Environment Regents Part B tests selection pressure constantly.

questions.prompt: A heritable trait that increases an organism's survival or reproductive success in its environment — thick fur, camouflage, finch beak shape.
questions.explanation: An adaptive trait is a heritable characteristic that improves an organism's fitness — survival and reproduction — in its environment. Classic examples: the long neck of a giraffe for reaching leaves; cryptic coloration in moths; antibiotic resistance in bacteria; salt tolerance in mangrove trees; sickle-cell heterozygote resistance to malaria. Adaptive traits arise by random mutation and become common through natural selection acting over generations. The same trait can be adaptive in one environment and harmful in another (thick fur on the tundra vs. desert). NY Living Environment Regents Part B asks students to identify adaptive traits in given scenarios and explain how natural selection drives the change.

questions.prompt: The comparison of preserved remains across rock layers — older species in deeper layers, transitional forms documenting major evolutionary changes.
questions.explanation: Fossil comparison places fossils from different rock layers in chronological order (older deeper, by the law of superposition) and looks for trends — gradual change in body size, limb structure, brain volume — across millions of years. Transitional fossils like Archaeopteryx (1861, between reptiles and birds), Tiktaalik (2004, between fish and tetrapods), and australopithecines (between earlier apes and modern humans) document major transitions. Whale evolution from land-mammal ancestors is documented by a fossil sequence spanning ~50 million years. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses fossil-comparison data tables and asks students to identify which species share more recent common ancestors.

questions.prompt: The similarity in DNA or protein sequences between two species — closer match means more recent common ancestor.
questions.explanation: Molecular similarity is the strongest modern evidence for evolutionary relatedness. Comparing the order of DNA bases or amino acids in protein sequences across species reveals which species share more recent common ancestors. Humans share ~98.8% of DNA with chimpanzees, ~85% with mice, ~60% with bananas. Cytochrome c (a protein used in cellular respiration) has 0 differences between humans and chimps, ~10 between humans and dogs, ~13 between humans and turtles, ~67 between humans and yeast — exactly matching what we'd expect from common ancestry. The NYSED Relationships and Biodiversity required lab uses simulated molecular data. NY Living Environment Regents Part B routinely uses sequence-comparison tables.

questions.prompt: Shifts in the variety of species in an ecosystem over time — caused by speciation, extinction, immigration, or major disturbance.
questions.explanation: Biodiversity changes over time through natural processes (speciation, extinction, ecological succession, climate change) and human activities (habitat loss, pollution, invasive species, climate change). The fossil record documents five major mass extinctions and many smaller ones, each followed by adaptive radiation of surviving lineages. The current biodiversity crisis — sometimes called the sixth mass extinction — is driven primarily by human activity. Conservation aims to slow biodiversity loss and restore lost diversity. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses biodiversity-change data in graphs and ecosystem-disturbance questions, asking students to identify causes and predict consequences of biodiversity change.

questions.prompt: The one-way movement of energy through an ecosystem — sunlight to producers to consumers, with ~90% lost as heat between levels.
questions.explanation: Energy flow through ecosystems is one-way: sunlight is captured by producers through photosynthesis, becomes chemical energy in biomass, and is passed up trophic levels as organisms eat each other. About 90% of energy is lost at each level — used for metabolism, lost as heat from cellular respiration, or in undigested waste — leaving only ~10% to the next level. This explains why food chains rarely exceed 4-5 links and why top predators are rare. Energy ultimately leaves the ecosystem as heat. This is fundamentally different from matter cycling, which recirculates. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses energy pyramids and the 10% rule constantly.

questions.prompt: The repeated reuse of elements (carbon, nitrogen, water, phosphorus) through ecosystems — producers, consumers, decomposers, atmosphere, soil.
questions.explanation: Matter cycles continuously through ecosystems — unlike energy, which flows one-way and dissipates. The main biogeochemical cycles are the water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff), the carbon cycle (photosynthesis fixes CO2, respiration releases it, fossil-fuel burning releases stored carbon), the nitrogen cycle (atmospheric N2 fixed to ammonia by bacteria, then nitrates, then back to atmosphere by denitrifying bacteria), and the phosphorus cycle. Disrupting these cycles — burning fossil fuels accelerating the carbon cycle, fertilizer runoff accelerating the nitrogen cycle — destabilizes ecosystems. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 uses cycle diagrams in human-impact questions.

questions.prompt: A line or bar graph showing population size over time — used to identify exponential growth, carrying capacity, and population crashes.
questions.explanation: Population graphs plot population size (y-axis) versus time (x-axis). Key patterns: exponential growth (J-curve, when resources are unlimited); logistic growth (S-curve, leveling off at carrying capacity); cyclical fluctuations (predator-prey oscillations); population crashes (mass die-offs after exceeding carrying capacity or environmental disaster); and stable populations. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 routinely shows a population graph and asks students to identify the carrying capacity (the plateau), explain why growth slows near it (limiting factors increase: competition, disease, food shortage), or predict the effect of a new predator or introduced species.

questions.prompt: A major change to an ecosystem — habitat loss, invasive species, climate shift, fire, flood — that causes cascading effects through food webs.
questions.explanation: An ecosystem disruption is any major change to an ecosystem's structure or function — drought, fire, flood, hurricane, volcanic eruption, deforestation, invasive species introduction, climate change. Disruptions cascade through food webs: removing one keystone species can cause population explosions or collapses in many others. The reintroduction of gray wolves to Yellowstone in 1995 reversed an earlier disruption (wolf extermination) — elk populations dropped, willows and aspens recovered, beaver populations rose, even stream channels stabilized. NY Living Environment Regents Part B-2 uses ecosystem-disruption scenarios in food-web questions, asking students to predict cascading effects of removing or adding a species.

questions.prompt: A planned approach to protecting and managing natural resources — sustainable harvest, protected areas, restoration, endangered-species laws.
questions.explanation: A conservation strategy combines science, law, and policy to protect and restore biodiversity and ecosystems. Tools include protected areas (national parks, wildlife refuges, marine reserves); sustainable harvest quotas (fishing limits, hunting seasons); endangered species laws (1973 U.S. Endangered Species Act); habitat restoration (replanting native forests, removing invasive species, dam removal); captive-breeding programs (California condor, black-footed ferret); wildlife corridors connecting fragmented habitats; and international agreements (CITES regulating trade in endangered species, the Paris climate agreement). Conservation involves tradeoffs with economic development, jobs, and indigenous rights. NY Living Environment Regents Part B uses conservation strategies in human-impact and biodiversity questions.

questions.prompt: The factor a scientist deliberately changes in an experiment — also called the manipulated variable.
questions.explanation: The independent variable is the factor the experimenter deliberately changes — the manipulated variable, the input. It is plotted on the x-axis. Example: testing the effect of light intensity on plant growth, light intensity is the independent variable. There is typically only one independent variable per experiment, with all other factors (controlled variables) held constant so any difference in outcome can be attributed to the IV. NY Living Environment Regents Part D required-lab questions and Part B-2 data-interpretation questions always test the student's ability to identify the IV — students must distinguish it from the DV (response) and the controlled variables (constants).

questions.prompt: The factor measured to test the hypothesis — also called the responding variable.
questions.explanation: The dependent variable is the factor the experimenter measures — the responding variable, the outcome that depends on the independent variable. It is plotted on the y-axis. In the plant-light-growth experiment, plant height in centimeters is the dependent variable. Good experimental design measures the DV with consistent, accurate, valid methods. NY Living Environment Regents Part D required-lab questions and Part B-2 data-interpretation questions always test the student's ability to identify the DV — students must distinguish it from the IV (the manipulated variable) and explain why measuring it tells them whether the IV had an effect.

questions.prompt: A factor held constant during the experiment so it does not interfere with measuring the effect of the independent variable.
questions.explanation: Controlled variables are factors held constant during an experiment so they cannot interfere with the measured effect of the independent variable. In the light-and-plant-growth experiment, controlled variables would include plant species, soil type, water volume, pot size, temperature, and duration — all kept identical across treatment groups. Without controlled variables, any observed effect could be due to one of these other factors rather than the IV being tested — the experiment is invalid. NY Living Environment Regents Part D required-lab questions routinely ask students to identify controlled variables and to explain why each one needs to be held constant for the experiment to be valid.

questions.prompt: A conclusion drawn directly from experimental data — supported by results, consistent with multiple repetitions.
questions.explanation: A valid conclusion is one supported directly by the experimental data — drawn from the measurements, not from assumptions or unrelated information. A valid conclusion should: (1) only describe what was tested (not generalize beyond the experiment); (2) be consistent with the data shown in tables and graphs; (3) state whether the hypothesis was supported or not supported (never 'proven'); (4) be reproducible — repeating the experiment yields similar results. Examples of invalid conclusions: claiming an effect that wasn't measured; ignoring counter-evidence; generalizing to species not tested. NY Living Environment Regents Part D regularly asks students to evaluate proposed conclusions and identify which are valid based on given data.

questions.prompt: A claim about the natural world supported directly by experimental evidence — the gold standard of science.
questions.explanation: A data-based claim is a statement about the natural world that is supported by experimental data — measurements, observations, statistical patterns. It is the gold standard of scientific reasoning: every conclusion must be tied back to the evidence, with the strength of the claim matching the strength of the data. Strong data-based claims: come from repeated, controlled experiments; specify both the effect and its size; quantify uncertainty; avoid overgeneralizing beyond what was tested. NY Living Environment Regents has emphasized data-based claims since the 2017 framework — Part B-2 and Part D CRQ items routinely ask students to make claims based on a data table or graph and to defend the claims with specific evidence from the data.
